The **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is the spontaneous, time-varying bioelectric potential recorded from the scalp, representing the synchronized postsynaptic activity of cortical pyramidal neurons. Its amplitude ranges from **5 µV to 300 µV** and frequency from **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz**.

The five standard EEG rhythms are:

| Rhythm | Frequency Range |
|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz |

**Keyword pointers for valuation:** (1) Mention *postsynaptic potentials of pyramidal cells* (1 mark), (2) amplitude range 5–300 µV (0.5 mark), (3) any 3 rhythms with frequency (1.5 marks).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** What is a motor unit? Define Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV) and state the normal motor NCV range.

**Model Answer:**

A **motor unit** is the basic functional unit of the neuromuscular system, comprising *one alpha motor neuron* (anterior horn cell) *and all the skeletal muscle fibers it innervates*. The number of fibers per motor unit varies (5 in extraocular muscles to 2000 in large postural muscles) and is inversely related to the precision of muscle control.

**Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV)** is the speed at which an action potential propagates along a peripheral nerve, given by:

$$
NCV = \dfrac{d}{t}
$$

where $d$ is the distance between stimulating and recording electrodes, and $t$ is the conduction latency.

**Normal motor NCV range = 50 to 60 m/s** (median, ulnar, tibial nerves).
**Normal sensory NCV range = 40 to 70 m/s.**

**Valuation key:** (1) Motor unit definition with example (1.5 marks), (2) Formula (1 mark), (3) Normal range (0.5 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING:**
> 1. *Do not* mix up **EEG amplitude (µV)** with **EMG amplitude (mV)** — examiners deduct heavily for unit confusion.
> 2. *Do not* confuse the *10–20 system* with *20–10* (reversed) — KTU 2024 question papers explicitly test this.
> 3. In NCV problems, *always convert units* to meters and seconds BEFORE substitution. Most students lose 2 marks by mixing mm with m.
> 4. In Spirometry, *always* mention whether the volume is measurable directly (TV, IRV, ERV, VC) or indirectly (RV, FRC, TLC).
> 5. In Pneumography, do NOT confuse *impedance pneumography* with *ECG* — although they share electrode sites, the underlying signals are different (kHz excitation vs. mHz ECG).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the EEG measurement system. Describe the 10–20 electrode placement system.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** List the five standard EEG rhythms. Explain the clinical significance of alpha and delta rhythms.

---

### Part (a) — Model Solution

**EEG Measurement System Block Diagram:**

```
[Brain Cortex] → [Electrodes (10-20 system, Ag/AgCl)] 
              → [Differential Amplifier (CMRR > 80 dB)]
              → [HPF (0.5 Hz)] → [LPF (30–70 Hz)] → [Notch (50 Hz)]
              → [Main Amplifier (Gain ~1000)]
              → [ADC (16 bit, ≥ 256 Hz)]
              → [Display & Storage]
```

**Explanation of each block:**

1. **Electrodes**: Surface Ag/AgCl disc electrodes convert ionic currents (from cortex) to electronic currents. Electrode-skin impedance < 5 kΩ ensured by skin preparation (alcohol cleaning, conductive gel).
2. **Differential Amplifier**: Amplifies only the *difference* between two scalp electrodes; rejects *common-mode* noise (50 Hz mains, body potentials). **CMRR > 80 dB** is required.
3. **Filtering**: 0.5 Hz HPF removes DC skin potentials; 30–70 Hz LPF removes EMG and RF interference; 50 Hz notch removes Indian mains interference.
4. **Main Amplifier**: Provides final gain (~1000×) to bring 50 µV EEG to 50 mV display range.
5. **ADC & Display**: Samples at 256 Hz or higher; 16-bit resolution; displayed on polygraph/monitor or stored on disk.

**10–20 Electrode Placement System:**

- Uses **21 electrodes** placed at **10% or 20%** of the nasion-inion and preauricular-preauricular distances.
- **Electrode Labels**:
  - **Fp1, Fp2** = Frontopolar
  - **F3, F4, F7, F8, Fz** = Frontal
  - **C3, C4, Cz** = Central
  - **P3, P4, Pz** = Parietal
  - **O1, O2** = Occipital
  - **T3, T4, T5, T6** = Temporal
- **Naming convention**: Odd numbers = **left hemisphere**, even numbers = **right hemisphere**, **"z"** = midline.
- The reference electrode is typically **A1, A2** (earlobes) or **Cz**.

**Mark split for part (a):** Block diagram (3 marks), explanation of each block (2 marks), 10-20 system description (2 marks).

---

### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Five Standard EEG Rhythms:**

| # | Rhythm | Frequency | Amplitude | State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | 20 – 200 µV | Deep sleep, coma, infants |
| 2 | Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz | 5 – 100 µV | Drowsiness, meditation |
| 3 | Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz | 20 – 200 µV | Awake, eyes closed, relaxed |
| 4 | Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz | 5 – 20 µV | Active thinking, alert |
| 5 | Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz | < 5 µV | Cognition, perception |

**Alpha Rhythm — Clinical Significance:**

- **Berger effect (Hans Berger, 1924)**: Alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz) is prominent in the **occipital region** when the subject is awake, relaxed, and eyes closed.
- **Attenuation (alpha blocking)**: When the eyes are *opened* or the subject engages in *mental effort* (e.g., arithmetic), the alpha rhythm *attenuates* (decreases in amplitude) and is replaced by beta waves (alpha desynchronization).
- **Clinical use**: Reduced or absent alpha in the awake relaxed state may indicate cerebral dysfunction; asymmetric alpha may indicate focal brain lesion.

**Delta Rhythm — Clinical Significance:**

- Delta waves (0.5 – 4 Hz) are normal in:
  - **Infants** (dominant rhythm in newborns).
  - **Deep sleep (Stage 3/4 NREM)** in adults.
- **Pathological delta** in awake adults indicates:
  - **Brain tumors, encephalopathy, traumatic brain injury, deep anesthesia**.
  - **Focal delta** is a strong indicator of underlying structural lesion.
- Delta activity is also used to confirm **brain death** — absence of delta/alpha in comatose patients is one of the criteria for electrocerebral silence.

**Mark split for part (b):** Five rhythms with table (3 marks), Alpha — clinical (2 marks), Delta — clinical (2 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternative]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** What is EMG? With a block diagram, explain the EMG measurement system. Compare surface and needle electrodes.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the procedure for measuring Nerve Conduction Velocity. A median nerve is stimulated at the wrist and the response is recorded from the abductor pollicis brevis at a distance of 210 mm with a latency of 3.8 ms. The same nerve is stimulated at the elbow 230 mm proximal to the wrist site, and the latency obtained is 8.2 ms. Calculate the motor nerve conduction velocity.

---

### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Definition of EMG:**

The **Electromyogram (EMG)** is the bioelectric signal recorded from skeletal muscle during voluntary or electrically-stimulated contraction, representing the spatial and temporal summation of Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs). Frequency range: **20 Hz to 5 kHz**; Amplitude range: **50 µV to 5 mV**.

**EMG Block Diagram:**

```
[Skeletal Muscle] → [Surface/Needle Electrodes]
                  → [Isolation Amplifier (Patient Safety)]
                  → [Instrumentation Amplifier (CMRR > 100 dB)]
                  → [HPF (20 Hz) → LPF (5 kHz) → Notch (50 Hz)]
                  → [Gain Stage (100–10000×)]
                  → [Rectifier & RMS Smoother]
                  → [ADC (12 bit, ≥ 1000 Hz)]
                  → [Display & Spectral Analysis]
```

**Comparison of Surface vs. Needle Electrodes:**

| Feature | Surface Electrode | Needle Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (penetrates skin) |
| **Recording area** | Large (many motor units) | Small (1–5 motor units) |
| **Selectivity** | Low | High |
| **Amplitude** | Low (100 µV – 5 mV) | High (50 µV – 5 mV) |
| **Discomfort** | None | Mild pain |
| **Application** | Kinesiology, ergonomics | Clinical diagnosis (myopathy, neuropathy) |
| **Types** | Ag/AgCl disc, flexible strip | Monopolar, concentric, bipolar |

**Mark split for part (a):** Definition with parameters (1.5 marks), Block diagram (3 marks), Surface vs. needle (2.5 marks).

---

### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Procedure for NCV Measurement:**

1. **Preparation**: Clean the skin with alcohol; apply surface electrodes (cathode over the nerve, anode distal).
2. **Stimulation**: Apply a supramaximal electrical pulse (constant current, typically 20–50 mA, 0.1–0.2 ms duration) to the nerve at point 1.
3. **Recording**: The Compound Muscle Action Potential (CMAP) is recorded by surface electrodes over the distal muscle (active muscle reference: muscle belly — tendon).
4. **Latency measurement**: Mark the time from stimulus onset to the *initial deflection* of the CMAP. This is the conduction latency $t_1$.
5. **Repeat at proximal site**: Move the stimulator proximally (e.g., elbow) and record new latency $t_2$. Measure inter-stimulus distance $\Delta d$.
6. **Compute NCV**:

$$
NCV = \dfrac{\Delta d}{t_2 - t_1}
$$

**Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:**
- Wrist-to-APB distance: $d_1 = 210\,\text{mm}$, latency $t_1 = 3.8\,\text{ms}$.
- Wrist-to-elbow distance: $\Delta d = 230\,\text{mm}$, elbow latency $t_2 = 8.2\,\text{ms}$.

**Step 1: Calculate the latency difference** (eliminates neuromuscular junction delay and terminal axon delay):

$$
\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 8.2\,\text{ms} - 3.8\,\text{ms} = 4.4\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2: Convert units to SI:**

$$
\Delta d = 230\,\text{mm} = 0.230\,\text{m} \\
\Delta t = 4.4\,\text{ms} = 4.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 0.0044\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute NCV:**

$$
NCV = \dfrac{0.230\,\text{m}}{0.0044\,\text{s}} = 52.27\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{NCV \approx 52.3\,\text{m/s}}
$$

**Clinical Interpretation:** The motor NCV of the median nerve is **52.3 m/s**, which is **within the normal range (50–60 m/s)**. No evidence of demyelination. If the value were < 40 m/s, demyelinating neuropathy (e.g., Carpal Tunnel Syndrome, Guillain-Barré) would be suspected.

**Mark split for part (b):** Procedure (3 marks), Numerical problem — given (1 mark), latency difference (1 mark), unit conversion (1 mark), final answer with interpretation (1 mark).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

### EEG Essentials
- ⭐ EEG amplitude: **5–300 µV** (microvolts, not millivolts).
- ⭐ EEG frequency: **0.5–100 Hz** (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma).
- ⭐ **Alpha** (8–13 Hz) = awake, eyes closed, occipital; **attenuates on eye opening (Berger effect)**.
- ⭐ **Delta** (0.5–4 Hz) = deep sleep / pathological in awake adults.
- ⭐ Origin = **postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons** (NOT action potentials).
- ⭐ **10–20 system** = 21 electrodes; odd = left, even = right, z = midline.
- ⭐ CMRR > **80 dB** required for EEG amplifier.
- ⭐ Evoked potentials require **$\sqrt{N}$ SNR improvement** by averaging *N* trials.

### EMG Essentials
- ⭐ Motor Unit = **1 alpha motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates**.
- ⭐ EMG amplitude: **50 µV – 5 mV** (100× larger than EEG).
- ⭐ EMG frequency: **20 Hz – 5 kHz** (much wider than EEG).
- ⭐ Electrodes: **Surface** (non-invasive, summed) vs **Needle** (invasive, selective).
- ⭐ Concentric needle is most common clinically.

### Nerve Conduction Velocity
- ⭐ $NCV = d \big/ t$ (distance ÷ latency).
- ⭐ Normal motor NCV: **50–60 m/s**.
- ⭐ Normal sensory NCV: **40–70 m/s**.
- ⭐ **Slowed NCV (< 40 m/s)** → demyelinating neuropathy.
- ⭐ **Reduced amplitude with normal NCV** → axonal neuropathy.
- ⭐ **Always convert units** to m and s before computation.

### Spirometry Essentials
- ⭐ Measures **static volumes** (TV, IRV, ERV, RV) and **capacities** (IC, FRC, VC, TLC).
- ⭐ TV = **500 mL**, RR = **12–20/min**, Minute Ventilation = **6–8 L/min** at rest.
- ⭐ RV, FRC, TLC **cannot be measured directly by spirometry** — require Helium dilution or body plethysmography.
- ⭐ Spirometer types: Water-sealed, dry rolling-seal, bellows, **pneumotachograph** (most modern).

### Pneumography Essentials
- ⭐ Measures **rate and waveform**, NOT volume (qualitative).
- ⭐ **Impedance pneumography** = most common; uses 50–100 kHz, 0.5–5 mA constant current.
- ⭐ Thoracic impedance change during breathing: **0.5–4 Ω** around baseline of 200–600 Ω.
- ⭐ Other types: Strain gauge, magnetometer, thermistor (nasal), inductive plethysmography (RIP).

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Don't confuse EEG µV with EMG mV (1000× error).
- ❌ Don't forget unit conversion in NCV problems.
- ❌ Don't claim RV/FRC/TLC are spirometric measurements — they require body plethysmography or He dilution.
- ❌ Don't write "impedance pneumography is the same as ECG" — they share electrodes but the *signal of interest* is different (kHz carrier vs. mHz QRS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Therapeutic Aid - Cardiac Pacemakers: Internal and External Pacemakers, Defibrillators, Ventilators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT742 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Therapeutic Aid - Cardiac Pacemakers: Internal and External Pacemakers, Defibrillators, Ventilators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Therapeutic Aids – The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therapeutic Aid Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A *therapeutic aid* is an active medical electronic device that delivers a controlled physiological stimulus (electrical, pneumatic, or mechanical) to a compromised organ system in order to **sustain, restore, or replace** a vital life function. Unlike diagnostic devices (which *measure*), therapeutic aids *intervene*.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3 of Biomedical Instrumentation), three classical therapeutic aids form the core of study:

| Therapeutic Aid | Organ Targeted | Energy Form Delivered |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardiac Pacemaker** | Heart (conduction system) | Pulsed electrical current |
| **Defibrillator** | Heart (myocardium in fibrillation) | High-energy DC shock |
| **Ventilator** | Lungs (alveolar gas exchange) | Positive/negative pressure air |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body as a City"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the human body as a busy city.
> - The **heart** is the power plant. A **pacemaker** is the back-up generator that keeps the turbines spinning at a fixed RPM when the plant's own controller fails.
> - When the turbines (heart muscle fibers) spin chaotically out of sync (fibrillation), a **defibrillator** delivers a massive "blackout-reset" jolt to synchronize them all at once.
> - The **lungs** are the city's air-circulation system. A **ventilator** is the industrial fan that mechanically pumps fresh air in and exhaust air out when the natural fans (diaphragm, intercostal muscles) stop working.

This city analogy is exactly how the KTU examiner frames viva questions — keep it ready.

## 1.3 Standard Physiological Metrics to Remember

The following constants are **universally accepted clinical reference values** and are frequently tested as 1-mark items:

- **Normal resting heart rate:** **60 – 100 beats per minute (bpm)**
- **Bradycardia threshold:** **< 60 bpm**
- **Tachycardia threshold:** **> 100 bpm**
- **Pacemaker pulse amplitude:** **3 – 5 V** (or 0.5 – 10 mA) into a **500 Ω** cardiac load
- **Pacemaker pulse width:** **0.5 – 2 ms**
- **Defibrillator energy (adult biphasic):** **120 – 200 J**; (monophasic) **360 J**
- **Normal tidal volume (V<sub>T</sub>):** **500 mL** (≈ 7 mL/kg body weight)
- **Normal respiratory rate (RR):** **12 – 20 breaths/min** for an adult
- **Inspiratory-to-Expiratory (I:E) ratio:** **1 : 2**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 CARDIAC PACEMAKERS — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Need for a Pacemaker

The sino-atrial (SA) node is the natural cardiac pacemaker. When it fails (Sick Sinus Syndrome, AV block), the ventricles may either:
1. Beat too slowly (bradycardia) — needs pacing.
2. Stop briefly (asystole / Stokes-Adams attacks) — needs demand pacing.

The artificial pacemaker supplies **sub-threshold-then-threshold pulsed current** to the myocardium, causing **artificial action potential propagation**.

### 2.1.2 Modes of Pacing — The NBG Code (5-letter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NASPE/BPEG Generic (NBG) Pacemaker Code** is the KTU-mandated way to describe any pacemaker. Memorize this table — questions are asked directly.

| Position | I | II | III | IV | V |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Chamber(s) Paced | Chamber(s) Sensed | Response to Sensing | Rate Modulation | Multisite Pacing |
| **Common codes** | A, V, D | A, V, D | I, T, D | R | A, V, D |
| **Legend** | A=Atrium, V=Ventricle, D=Dual | T=Triggered, I=Inhibited, D=Dual | R=Rate-adaptive | O=None |

**Most clinically common:** **DDD** (Dual-chamber, Dual-sensed, Dual-response) and **VVI** (Ventricular demand, the classical workhorse).

### 2.1.3 Internal vs External Pacemaker — Engineering Comparison

| Parameter | **External (Transcutaneous / Temporary)** | **Internal (Implantable / Permanent)** |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | AC mains / 9 V battery | **Lithium-iodine** battery (life 5–12 yrs) |
| Electrodes | Skin-surface pads (large area) | Endocardial (transvenous) or epicardial leads |
| Pulse amplitude | 20 – 200 mA (high due to skin impedance) | 0.5 – 10 mA (low — direct contact) |
| Pulse width | 1 – 5 ms | 0.5 – 2 ms |
| Patient mobility | Confined to bed | Fully ambulatory |
| Indication | Emergency, post-cardiac surgery | Chronic AV block, SSS |
| Sensing | Usually asynchronous | Synchronous (demand) with amplifier |

### 2.1.4 Pacemaker Block Diagram

The functional architecture (detailed Mermaid version in Section 4) consists of:

1. **Power source** (battery + DC-DC converter)
2. **Timing / pulse generator circuit** (astable multivibrator, typically CMOS 555 or microcontroller)
3. **Output stage** (capacitor discharge through patient via constant-voltage or constant-current source)
4. **Sensing amplifier** (ECG amplifier, gain ≈ 1000, bandwidth 20–200 Hz)
5. **Logic / refractory circuit** (blanking interval 200–400 ms to avoid T-wave sensing)
6. **Telemetry coil** (for internal pacemakers — bidirectional communication with programmer)

### 2.1.5 Demand Pacemaker — Why and How

A demand pacemaker **inhibits** its output when it senses a spontaneous R-wave (intrinsic beat) faster than the set lower rate limit (LRL). This avoids the dangerous "R-on-T" phenomenon, which can trigger ventricular fibrillation.

**Lower Rate Limit (LRL) formula:**

$$f_{LRL} = \frac{60}{T_{escape}} \quad \text{[beats per minute]}$$

where $T_{escape}$ is the escape interval in seconds.

---

## 2.2 DEFIBRILLATORS — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Need for a Defibrillator

When the myocardium fibrillates (ventricular fibrillation – VF, or pulseless ventricular tachycardia – pVT), the cardiac muscle fibers contract in a chaotic, disorganized manner — **no coordinated pumping, no cardiac output, no pulse**. Death occurs within minutes.

A defibrillator delivers a **brief, high-energy DC shock** that **depolarizes a critical mass of myocardium simultaneously**, allowing the SA node to re-establish sinus rhythm.

### 2.2.2 Types of Defibrillators

1. **External (manual / AED)** — paddles/pads on chest.
2. **Internal (ICD)** — Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator; placed subcutaneously like a pacemaker, with leads in the right ventricle.
3. **Monophasic** — current flows in one direction only. Older technology.
4. **Biphasic** — current reverses polarity midway. **Modern standard**, requires less energy and causes less myocardial damage.

### 2.2.3 Defibrillator Block Diagram & Energy Delivery

The energy is stored in a large capacitor and then discharged through the patient via an inductor (in modern biphasic units the inductor shapes the waveform). The **delivered energy** is given by:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

where $C$ = capacitance (Farads), $V$ = charging voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard internal capacitor values are **16 – 50 µF** (typically **32 µF** in adult units). Charging voltage is **1500 – 2500 V DC** to deliver 200–360 J into a 50 Ω patient impedance.

### 2.2.4 The Delivered vs Stored Energy Distinction

$$E_{delivered} = E_{stored} \times \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \quad \text{for a capacitive discharge}$$

But in practice, the energy *actually delivered* to the patient is roughly **65–80%** of the stored energy because of internal resistive losses in the paddles/lead wires. The KTU exam frequently asks this difference.

### 2.2.5 Synchronized Cardioversion

When the patient has a tachyarrhythmia (e.g., atrial flutter, SVT) with a pulse, the shock must be **synchronized to the R-wave** of the QRS to avoid hitting the vulnerable T-wave period (which would *cause* VF). The synchronization circuit introduces a **25–40 ms delay** to fire the SCR/IGBT precisely on the R-peak.

---

## 2.3 VENTILATORS — Theory of Operation

### 2.3.1 Need for a Ventilator

A ventilator is required when the patient cannot maintain adequate alveolar ventilation, due to:
- **Neuromuscular failure** (Guillain-Barré, polio, spinal injury)
- **CNS depression** (drug overdose, brain injury)
- **Pulmonary failure** (ARDS, severe pneumonia, COPD exacerbation)
- **Anesthesia** (during surgery)

### 2.3.2 Classification by Pressure / Flow Control

| Mode | Controlling Variable | Cycled By | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume-Controlled (VCV)** | Tidal Volume (V<sub>T</sub>) | Volume (or time) | Setting V<sub>T</sub>=500 mL, RR=12 |
| **Pressure-Controlled (PCV)** | Airway Pressure (P<sub>aw</sub>) | Pressure (or time) | Setting P<sub>insp</sub>=20 cmH₂O |
| **Pressure Support (PSV)** | Pressure | Patient-triggered (flow) | Augments spontaneous breaths |
| **PEEP** | Positive End-Expiratory Pressure | — | Prevents alveolar collapse |
| **SIMV** | Mix | Synchronized to patient effort | Weaning mode |

### 2.3.3 Mandatory Ventilator Parameters (Set by Clinician)

- **Tidal Volume (V<sub>T</sub>):** Volume of air per breath → 6–8 mL/kg ideal body weight
- **Respiratory Rate (RR / f):** Breaths per minute → 12–20
- **Fraction of Inspired Oxygen (FiO₂):** 0.21 (room air) to 1.0 (pure O₂)
- **PEEP:** 5–15 cmH₂O
- **I:E ratio:** 1:2 (default)
- **Inspiratory flow rate:** 30–60 L/min

### 2.3.4 Minute Ventilation Equation — The Master Equation

$$\dot{V}_{E} = V_T \times f \quad \text{[L/min]}$$

Normal $\dot{V}_{E}$ ≈ 5–8 L/min for a healthy adult. The ventilator must deliver this regardless of patient effort.

### 2.3.5 Alveolar Gas Equation (Optional but High-Yield)

$$P_AO_2 = F_iO_2 \times (P_{atm} - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_aCO_2}{R}$$

where $P_{atm} = 760$ mmHg, $P_{H_2O} = 47$ mmHg, $R \approx 0.8$.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat Sheet** — memorize for ESE.

| # | Formula / Constant | Use | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Defibrillator stored energy | 32 µF, 2000 V → **64 J** |
| 2 | $E_{delivered} \approx 0.7 E_{stored}$ | Real delivered energy | 360 J set → ~250 J delivered (mono) |
| 3 | $f_{LRL} = 60 / T_{escape}$ | Pacemaker lower rate | $T_{esc}=1$ s → 60 bpm |
| 4 | $R_{patient} \approx 50 \, \Omega$ | Thoracic impedance (defib) | Pads must be well-gelled |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_E = V_T \times f$ | Minute ventilation | 500 mL × 12 = **6 L/min** |
| 6 | $P_AO_2$ equation | Alveolar O₂ partial pressure | ~100 mmHg on room air |
| 7 | **NBG Code (DDD/VVI/AAI)** | Pacemaker mode | Most common: **DDD** |
| 8 | I:E ratio | Inspiration:Expiration | **1:2** |
| 9 | Pacemaker pulse width | Stimulation efficiency | 0.5–2 ms |
| 10 | Battery life (Li-I₂) | Implantable pacemaker | **5–12 years** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Defibrillator Stored Energy for a Given Delivered Dose

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):** A defibrillator uses a 32 µF capacitor. The patient impedance is 50 Ω. The clinician needs 200 J of *delivered* energy. Find the required charging voltage. Assume the discharge through the patient is a critically-damped RC circuit and 70% of stored energy reaches the patient.

**Step 1 — Identify the relation between stored and delivered energy.**

For a single exponential RC discharge into a resistive load, the total energy dissipated in the load equals the energy stored in the capacitor (no other sinks). However, real defibrillators have internal losses in the inductor, switches, and lead resistance, so we model the patient share as:

$$E_{delivered} = \eta \cdot E_{stored}$$

with $\eta = 0.70$ (typical KTU textbook value).

**Step 2 — Express stored energy.**

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$E_{delivered} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_c$.**

$$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{delivered}}{\eta C}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200}{0.70 \times 32 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{400}{22.4 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{17.857 \times 10^{6}} \\
&\approx 4226 \, \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Result.**

$$\boxed{V_c \approx 4.23 \, \text{kV}}$$

**Step 7 — Time constant check (5τ rule for "complete" discharge).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= R C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 1.6 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

5τ = 8 ms — typical defibrillation pulse duration matches this. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lower Rate Limit of a Demand Pacemaker

**Problem:** A demand pacemaker has an escape interval timing capacitor and resistor. The timing circuit is an RC astable multivibrator with $R_T = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$ and $C_T = 1.0 \, \mu F$. Find the LRL in bpm.

**Step 1 — Recall 555-timer astable frequency.**

For a 555 astable in pacemaker timing:

$$T = 0.693 \, R_T C_T \quad \text{(per half cycle, for one timing interval)}$$

For the full escape interval (the relevant one for LRL):

$$T_{escape} = 1.386 \, R_T C_T$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{escape} &= 1.386 \times 100 \times 10^{3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 0.1386 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bpm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{LRL} &= \frac{60}{T_{escape}} = \frac{60}{0.1386} \\
&\approx 433 \, \text{bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

This is far too high for a cardiac pacemaker (physiological max ≈ 180 bpm). Hence real pacemakers use **countdown digital timers** or **higher R, lower C** combinations. The examiner wants you to comment on this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 555-astable alone is unsuitable for a modern pacemaker. Modern devices use **CMOS microcontroller-based timing** with crystal reference for accuracy, or **digital countdown dividers** driven by a 32.768 kHz watch crystal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minute Ventilation Requirement on a Ventilator

**Problem:** A 70 kg adult patient is on volume-control ventilation. The clinician sets V<sub>T</sub> = 7 mL/kg and RR = 14. Find the minute ventilation and compare with the normal physiological range.

**Step 1 — Compute tidal volume.**

$$V_T = 7 \, \text{mL/kg} \times 70 \, \text{kg} = 490 \, \text{mL}$$

**Step 2 — Compute minute ventilation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_E &= V_T \times f = 490 \times 14 \\
&= 6860 \, \text{mL/min} = 6.86 \, \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to normal.**

Normal $\dot{V}_E$ for an adult at rest: 5–8 L/min. Result lies in range. ✓

**Step 4 — Calculate I:E ratio and timing.**

With I:E = 1:2 and RR = 14:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= \frac{60}{14} = 4.286 \, \text{s} \\
T_{insp} &= \frac{1}{3} T_{total} = 1.43 \, \text{s} \\
T_{exp} &= \frac{2}{3} T_{total} = 2.86 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute peak inspiratory flow (for square-wave pattern).**

$$
\dot{V}_{insp} = \frac{V_T}{T_{insp}} = \frac{0.490}{1.43} \approx 0.343 \, \text{L/s} = 20.6 \, \text{L/min}
$$

For **decelerating flow** (more physiological), the peak is ≈ 2× this average. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing Therapeutic Aid

| Patient Condition | Bradycardia (HR < 60) | VF / Pulseless VT (chaotic) | Apnea / Respiratory failure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pulse present?** | Yes (slow) | **No** | Varies |
| **Consciousness** | Usually conscious | Unconscious | Unconscious / sedated |
| **Therapeutic Aid** | **Pacemaker** (transcutaneous → transvenous → permanent) | **Defibrillator** (AED → manual biphasic → ICD) | **Ventilator** (NIV → invasive) |
| **Energy/Stimulus** | 0.5–10 mA pulses, 0.5–2 ms | 120–360 J DC shock (biphasic < monophasic) | 6–8 mL/kg V<sub>T</sub>, 12–20 bpm |
| **Time-criticality** | Hours to days (elective permanent) | **Seconds** (defib within 3–5 min) | Minutes to hours |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal (Implantable) Pacemaker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Li-I2 Battery 2.8V] --> B[DC-DC Voltage Multiplier]
    B --> C[Timing Circuit 32.768 kHz Crystal]
    C --> D[Microcontroller / Logic Unit]
    D --> E[Output Pulse Generator]
    E --> F[Output Coupling Capacitor]
    F --> G[Lead Tip to Myocardium]
    H[Sensing Amplifier] --> D
    I[Intrinsic ECG Electrode] --> H
    J[Telemetry Coil] <--> D
    K[Refractory Timer 300ms] --> D
    L[Magnet Reed Switch] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The battery charges via DC-DC multiplier. The crystal + microcontroller generate precisely-timed pulses. The output capacitor delivers the stimulating current to the heart. The sensing amplifier "listens" for intrinsic R-waves; if detected, the refractory timer blanks out the next 300 ms to prevent T-wave sensing. The telemetry coil allows the cardiologist to reprogram the device non-invasively.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — DC Defibrillator (Biphasic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains 230V] --> B[High-Voltage Transformer]
    B --> C[HV Rectifier]
    C --> D[Storage Capacitor 32 uF]
    D --> E[Charge Indicator]
    D --> F[Inductor 25 mH]
    F --> G[Patient Electrode Pads]
    G --> H[Sync Circuit R-wave Detector]
    H --> I[SCR or IGBT Switch]
    I --> F
    J[Control Panel] --> I
    J --> K[Energy Selector J]
    K --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical safety node:** The **sync circuit** is the difference between life-saving and life-threatening. For VF, it is bypassed (asynchronous discharge). For cardioversion of SVT/atrial flutter, it MUST detect the R-wave and fire the SCR on the R-peak to avoid the vulnerable T-wave zone.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Positive-Pressure Mechanical Ventilator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Air O2 Blender] --> B[Inspiratory Line]
    B --> C[Flow Sensor]
    C --> D[Inspiratory Valve]
    D --> E[Patient Airway via ET Tube]
    E --> F[Expiratory Line]
    F --> G[Expiratory Valve PEEP]
    G --> H[Room Air Outlet]
    E --> I[Pressure Sensor]
    I --> J[Control CPU]
    C --> J
    J --> K[Display Alarm Module]
    J --> D
    J --> G
    L[Operator Settings V_T RR FiO2 PEEP] --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional reading:** The blender mixes air and oxygen to the set FiO₂. The flow sensor and pressure sensor feed back to the control CPU, which actuates the inspiratory and expiratory valves. PEEP is maintained by an expiratory threshold resistor. All safety alarms (high pressure, low V<sub>T</sub>, apnea) are derived from these sensors.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selection of Therapeutic Aid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Patient Assessment] --> Q1{Heart rhythm?}
    Q1 -->|Slow HR or block| P[Cardiac Pacemaker]
    Q1 -->|Chaotic or absent| Q2{Pulse present?}
    Q1 -->|Normal rhythm| Q3{Respiration OK?}
    Q2 -->|No| D[Defibrillator Emergency]
    Q2 -->|Yes synchronized| SC[Synchronized Cardioversion]
    Q3 -->|No| V[Ventilator]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Obs[Observation only]
    P --> P1[External Temporary]
    P --> P2[Permanent Implantable]
    D --> D1[AED]
    D --> D2[Manual Biphasic]
    V --> V1[Non-invasive NIV]
    V --> V2[Invasive ET Tube]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between internal and external pacemakers on the basis of (i) power source, (ii) electrode placement, and (iii) clinical indication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Internal Pacemaker | External Pacemaker |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Power source** | Lithium-iodine battery (implanted, life 5–12 years) | AC mains or 9 V external battery |
| **(ii) Electrode placement** | Transvenous (endocardial) tip in right ventricle, or epicardial leads on heart surface | Skin-surface adhesive pads on chest |
| **(iii) Clinical indication** | Chronic AV block, Sick Sinus Syndrome — long-term therapy | Emergency bradycardia, post-cardiac-surgery temporary pacing |

*(1 mark per row — Examiner's note: students often forget to specify "lithium-iodine" for the battery type, costing 0.5 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the NBG pacemaker code "VVI". What does each letter signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **V** — Chamber **Paced**: **V**entricle
- **V** — Chamber **Sensed**: **V**entricle
- **I** — Response to sensing: **I**nhibited (output suppressed when intrinsic R-wave is sensed)

A VVI pacemaker therefore paces only the ventricle, senses the ventricle, and inhibits its output when it detects a spontaneous ventricular beat. *(2 marks for explanation + 1 mark for correct meaning of each letter.)*

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **implantable (internal) demand pacemaker**. Mention the function of the sensing amplifier and the refractory period.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A defibrillator uses a 32 µF capacitor. Calculate the charging voltage required to deliver 200 J to a 50 Ω patient, assuming 70% of the stored energy is delivered. Also find the RC time constant of the discharge.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**1. Block diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1. Components: battery, DC-DC converter, timing circuit, output stage, sensing amplifier, refractory timer, telemetry coil.

**2. Working explanation (3 marks):**
- The Li-I₂ battery (~2.8 V) is boosted by a DC-DC converter to ~5 V for digital logic.
- A crystal oscillator (32.768 kHz) drives a microcontroller that times the escape interval (e.g., 1000 ms for 60 bpm).
- When the escape interval expires without a sensed R-wave, the output stage discharges a coupling capacitor (0.5–10 mA, 0.5–2 ms pulse) into the lead tip in the right ventricle.
- This depolarizes the myocardium, triggering a paced beat.
- If a spontaneous R-wave is sensed within the escape interval, the timing is reset and no pulse is delivered — hence "**demand**" mode.

**3. Sensing amplifier & refractory period (1 mark):**
- The sensing amplifier is a high-gain (~1000), bandpass-filtered (20–200 Hz) ECG amplifier connected to the same lead.
- After every sensed or paced event, a **refractory timer** blanks the input for **200–400 ms** to prevent the device from sensing the T-wave or the pacing artifact itself as an "intrinsic" beat, which would otherwise lead to runaway inhibition.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Use stored/delivered energy relation:**

$$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{delivered}}{\eta C}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200}{0.70 \times 32 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{400}{22.4 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{17.857 \times 10^{6}} \\
&\approx \boxed{4226 \, \text{V} \approx 4.23 \, \text{kV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — RC time constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= R C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= \boxed{1.6 \, \text{ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion (1 mark):**
For near-complete discharge, ~5τ ≈ 8 ms is required. This matches the typical defibrillation pulse width, confirming the engineering choice. Biphasic waveforms further optimize this.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **biphasic DC defibrillator** with a block diagram. Differentiate it from a monophasic defibrillator.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A patient is on volume-control ventilation with V<sub>T</sub> = 8 mL/kg, body weight = 60 kg, RR = 15, and I:E = 1:2. Calculate (i) tidal volume, (ii) minute ventilation, (iii) inspiratory time, and (iv) average inspiratory flow rate.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**1. Block diagram (3 marks):**
See Section 4.2. AC mains → HV transformer → HV rectifier → storage capacitor → patient via inductor → SCR/IGBT switch → back to capacitor with reversed polarity for phase 2.

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- AC mains is stepped up to ~2000 V and rectified to charge the 32 µF storage capacitor.
- The capacitor is charged to a voltage corresponding to the selected energy (e.g., 200 J → ~4000 V).
- The energy is delivered through an inductor (25 mH) that shapes the waveform.
- **Monophasic:** Current flows in one direction only (one polarity) for the entire pulse (~4–12 ms).
- **Biphasic:** Current reverses direction mid-pulse. Typically phase 1 is 60% of the duration at one polarity, phase 2 is 40% at opposite polarity. This requires less total energy (~120–200 J vs 360 J) for equivalent defibrillation success and causes **less myocardial damage**.

**3. Comparison table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Energy for adult VF | 360 J | 120–200 J |
| Current direction | One | Two (reversed mid-pulse) |
| Myocardial injury | Higher | Lower |
| Efficacy in high-impedance patients | Lower | Higher |
| Modern standard | Obsolete | **Yes (current standard)** |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Given:** V<sub>T</sub>/kg = 8 mL/kg, weight = 60 kg, RR = f = 15, I:E = 1:2.

**(i) Tidal volume:**

$$V_T = 8 \times 60 = \boxed{480 \, \text{mL}}$$

**(ii) Minute ventilation:**

$$
\dot{V}_E = V_T \times f = 480 \times 15 = 7200 \, \text{mL/min} = \boxed{7.2 \, \text{L/min}}
$$

**(iii) Inspiratory time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= \frac{60}{15} = 4.0 \, \text{s} \\
T_{insp} &= \frac{1}{1+2} \times 4.0 = \frac{4}{3} = \boxed{1.33 \, \text{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Average inspiratory flow rate:**

$$
\dot{V}_{insp} = \frac{V_T}{T_{insp}} = \frac{0.480 \, \text{L}}{1.33 \, \text{s}} = \boxed{0.36 \, \text{L/s} \approx 21.6 \, \text{L/min}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Students frequently write mL/min for L/min or forget to convert seconds to minutes. Always carry the unit and convert at the END.
> 2. **I:E ratio confusion:** I:E = 1:2 means inspiration is **1 part**, expiration is **2 parts**, total cycle = 3 parts. Many students incorrectly treat 1:2 as "inspiration takes 1 second, expiration takes 2 seconds" without scaling to the cycle time. Always compute the cycle time first.
> 3. **Defibrillator voltage:** Students often write the answer in V instead of kV. With 32 µF and 200 J, the answer is in the **kilovolt range** — keep the unit visible.
> 4. **Refractory period confusion:** Some students write "300 ms blanking" for pacemakers without specifying *why*. Always mention: "to prevent T-wave sensing" — examiner gives the second sub-mark only when this is stated.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Cardiac Pacemaker** = electrical pulse generator. *Internal* = implanted, Li-I₂ battery, leads inside heart. *External* = pads on chest, used in emergencies.
- **NBG Code (5-letter):** Chamber-Paced, Chamber-Sensed, Response, Rate-adaptive, Multisite. Most common: **DDD, VVI**.
- **Demand mode** = inhibited by sensed intrinsic beat. **Asynchronous (fixed-rate)** = no sensing — used only when no intrinsic activity exists.
- **Refractory blanking** of 200–400 ms is essential to prevent T-wave sensing.
- **Lower Rate Limit:** $f_{LRL} = 60 / T_{escape}$ bpm.
- **Defibrillator** delivers a brief high-energy DC shock. Capacitor energy: $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$.
- **Biphasic > Monophasic** in modern practice — lower energy, less injury, better for high-impedance patients.
- **Synchronized cardioversion** is mandatory when the patient has a pulse (shock on R-wave). **Asynchronous defibrillation** is for pulseless VF/VT.
- **Ventilator** delivers positive-pressure breaths. Modes: VCV, PCV, PSV, PEEP, SIMV.
- **Minute Ventilation:** $\dot{V}_E = V_T \times f$ — typically 5–8 L/min normal.
- **I:E ratio** default **1:2**. **V<sub>T</sub>** default **6–8 mL/kg** IBW.
- **Alveolar gas equation** is the high-yield physiology behind ventilator FiO₂ settings.
- **Safety alarms** on all three devices are derived from sensors (ECG amplitude, paddle impedance, airway pressure) — never disable them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advances in Radiological Imaging - X-rays: Principles of Generation, Uses of X-rays - Diagnostic Still Picture, Fluoroscopy, Angiography, Endoscopy, and Diathermy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT742 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advances in Radiological Imaging - X-rays: Principles of Generation, Uses of X-rays - Diagnostic Still Picture, Fluoroscopy, Angiography, Endoscopy, and Diathermy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of X-rays

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** **X-rays** are a form of **ionizing electromagnetic radiation** with photon energies in the range of **100 eV to 100 keV** (diagnostic range typically 20–150 keV) and wavelengths of approximately **0.01 nm to 10 nm** ($1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$). They are produced by the sudden deceleration of high-velocity electrons when they strike a metallic target inside an evacuated tube.

X-rays occupy the high-frequency end of the electromagnetic spectrum, between **ultraviolet light** and **gamma radiation**. They were discovered in **1895 by Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen**, who received the first Nobel Prize in Physics (1901) for this discovery — making it a landmark question in KTU viva voce sessions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Properties That Make X-rays Clinically Useful:**
> 1. **Penetrating Power** – They can pass through soft tissue but are differentially absorbed by bone, metal, and contrast media.
> 2. **Ionizing Nature** – They can ionize atoms and damage DNA (basis for both diagnostic imaging and radiotherapy).
> 3. **Photochemical Effect** – They expose photographic films and excite fluorescent screens (image formation).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Hospital Flashlight"**
> Imagine X-rays as a special kind of "super flashlight" for doctors. A normal torchlight bounces off the surface of your skin and shows you only the outer skin. But an X-ray flashlight is so energetic that it can **shine right through soft body tissue (skin, muscle, fat)** but gets **blocked by dense structures (bones, teeth, metal implants)**. The X-ray machine works like taking a "shadow photograph" of your insides, where bones cast a darker shadow on the film because they absorb more X-rays than the surrounding muscle. This is exactly how a regular shadow is formed when sunlight passes around your hand onto a wall — except X-rays see through flesh, not cardboard.

## 1.3 Categorization of X-ray Uses

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the medical applications under five heads. Let us preview them before deep-diving in Section 2.

| Application | Type | Output |
|-------------|------|--------|
| Diagnostic Still Picture (Radiography) | Static imaging | Single 2-D shadow image on film/detector |
| Fluoroscopy | Real-time dynamic imaging | Continuous live X-ray video on monitor |
| Angiography | Contrast-enhanced vascular imaging | Blood vessel silhouette on image |
| Endoscopy | Internal cavity inspection (X-ray assisted) | Fiber-optic/RF-assisted visualization |
| Diathermy | Therapeutic tissue heating / surgical cutting | Heat-induced tissue modification (uses HF current, not photons — see §1.4) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diathermy vs. X-rays:** Strictly speaking, **Diathermy** does **not** use X-rays. It uses **high-frequency alternating current (HFAC, typically 0.3–5 MHz)** passed through body tissue, causing rapid ionic oscillation and resistive heating. It is grouped with X-ray topics in the syllabus because it is part of the broader "electromagnetic/radiological energy used in medicine" cluster. We will treat it as such in §2.5.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of X-ray Generation

X-rays are produced inside an **X-ray tube** (also called a **Coolidge tube**, after its inventor William D. Coolidge, 1913). The generation process has **four sequential stages**.

### Stage 1: Thermionic Emission
- A **tungsten filament** (cathode) is heated to a temperature of approximately **2,500 K (2,227 °C)** by passing a low-voltage heating current (typically 3–5 A at 10 V).
- At this temperature, the kinetic energy of the electrons exceeds the **work function ($\phi$)** of tungsten ($\phi \approx 4.5 \text{ eV}$).
- Electrons "boil off" the filament surface — this is **thermionic emission**.

### Stage 2: Electron Acceleration
- A **high potential difference (kVp)** of 30 kV to 150 kV is applied between the cathode (–) and the anode (+).
- Electrons are accelerated to approximately **½ the speed of light**.
- The **kinetic energy of an electron at the anode** equals the work done on it by the electric field:

$$E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

where $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ is the electron charge, $V$ is the tube potential in volts, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$ is the electron rest mass, and $v$ is the electron velocity.

### Stage 3: Sudden Deceleration at the Anode
- The accelerated electrons strike a **rotating tungsten (W, Z=74) target** anode, sometimes alloyed with **rhenium (Re)** for thermal ruggedness.
- About **99 % of the electron kinetic energy is converted to heat** — only **~1 %** is converted to X-ray photons. This is why anode cooling is critical.
- The remaining 1 % is converted to X-rays through **two mechanisms**:

#### (a) Bremsstrahlung ("Braking Radiation") — Continuous Spectrum
- When a high-speed electron passes **close to the nucleus** of a target atom, the strong Coulomb attraction **decelerates** and **deflects** the electron.
- The lost kinetic energy is emitted as an X-ray photon.
- The energy of each photon can be **any value from zero up to the full kinetic energy** of the incident electron, producing a **continuous spectrum**.

#### (b) Characteristic Radiation — Discrete Spectrum
- When the incident electron has enough energy to **eject an inner-shell electron** (typically the K-shell, binding energy ~70 keV for W) from the target atom, an outer-shell electron drops into the vacancy.
- The energy difference between the shells is emitted as a photon of **discrete, characteristic energy** (e.g., $K_\alpha$, $K_\beta$ lines for tungsten).
- This produces **sharp peaks** superimposed on the continuous Bremsstrahlung spectrum.

### Stage 4: X-ray Emission
- X-ray photons leave the tube through a **beryllium (Be) window**, chosen because Be has a very low atomic number (Z=4) and therefore low X-ray absorption, allowing soft (low-energy) X-rays to pass through with minimal filtration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Rotating Anode?**
> In modern diagnostic tubes, the anode is a disc rotated at ~3,000–10,000 RPM by an induction motor. The electron beam strikes a moving focal track, distributing the heat over a much larger area than a stationary anode would allow — this permits higher tube currents and shorter exposure times.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favourite — Memorize This Table.** A minimum of one 3-mark Part A question in every KTU ESE since 2019 has been a direct formula recall or short derivation from this table.

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | Meaning of Each Symbol | Typical Units |
|---|---------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h f = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | $h$ = Planck's constant ($6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s), $f$ = frequency, $c$ = $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\lambda$ = wavelength | $E$ in J or eV, $\lambda$ in m |
| 2 | Duane–Hunt Law (Cut-off) | $h f_{\max} = e V$ | $f_{\max}$ = maximum photon frequency, $e$ = $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V$ = tube voltage | Hz, V |
| 3 | Minimum Wavelength | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{h c}{e V} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ V·m}$ | Same as above | metres (m) |
| 4 | Attenuation (Beer–Lambert Law) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | $I_0$ = incident intensity, $I$ = transmitted intensity, $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient, $x$ = thickness | $I, I_0$ in W/m², $\mu$ in m⁻¹, $x$ in m |
| 5 | Mass Attenuation Coefficient | $\mu_m = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\rho$ = density of absorber | m²/kg |
| 6 | Half-Value Layer (HVL) | $x_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | Thickness that reduces intensity to half | m or mm |
| 7 | X-ray Intensity vs. Tube Current | $I \propto i \cdot Z \cdot V^{2}$ | $i$ = tube current (mA), $Z$ = atomic number of target, $V$ = tube voltage (kV) | qualitative |
| 8 | Power Dissipated as Heat at Anode | $P_{\text{heat}} = V \cdot i$ (≈ 99 % of input) | $V$ = tube voltage, $i$ = tube current | watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Reminder:** In all KTU answers, **never write $eV$ ambiguously**. State clearly: "$eV$ = energy of one electron accelerated through 1 volt = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, also known as 1 electron-volt (eV)." Examiners deduct ½ mark for sloppy units.

## 2.3 Use 1: Diagnostic Still Picture (Radiography)

This is the most common X-ray application — a **static shadow image** of internal body structures, captured on **photographic film** or a **digital flat-panel detector (FPD)**.

**Mechanism:**
- X-ray beam is collimated into a narrow, well-defined cone.
- Patient is positioned between source and detector.
- Differential absorption: **bone $\mu \approx 2$ cm⁻¹**, **muscle $\mu \approx 0.2$ cm⁻¹**, **air $\mu \approx 0$ cm⁻¹**.
- Transmitted beam darkens film (silver-halide emulsion) at locations of low absorption.

**Key parameters:** **kVp** controls *penetration* (beam quality), **mAs** controls *quantity* (number of photons) and therefore image density.

## 2.4 Use 2: Fluoroscopy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluoroscopy = Real-time X-ray "movie".** Used to observe dynamic processes like swallowing, catheter placement, or angioplasty.

A standard fluoroscopy chain:
1. **X-ray tube** produces continuous low-dose beam.
2. **Image intensifier (II) tube** amplifies the faint X-ray image by ~1,000–5,000× using a **photocathode** + **electrostatic lens** + **output phosphor**.
3. The brightened image is captured by a **CCD/CMOS camera** and displayed on a **TV monitor** in real time.

**Patient dose in fluoroscopy is much higher than in radiography** — a typical abdominal fluoroscopy gives 5–15 mGy/min, whereas a single chest X-ray gives ~0.02 mGy.

## 2.5 Use 3: Angiography

**Angiography = X-ray imaging of blood vessels** (Greek: *angeion* = vessel, *graphein* = to write).

- A **radiopaque contrast medium** (iodine-based, e.g., Iohexol; Z=53, K-edge 33.2 keV) is injected into the blood vessel.
- Iodine's high atomic number and K-edge near the diagnostic X-ray energy range causes strong attenuation, making vessels visible as dark "negative" shadows.
- **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** is the gold standard: a pre-contrast "mask" image is digitally subtracted from the contrast-filled image, removing bone and soft tissue and leaving only the vessels.

**Sub-types:** Coronary angiography, cerebral angiography, peripheral angiography, CT angiography (CTA).

## 2.6 Use 4: Endoscopy

> [!NOTE]
> **Endoscopy = direct visual examination of the interior of a hollow organ or cavity** using a flexible or rigid tube called an *endoscope*. The word comes from Greek *endo* (within) + *skopein* (to look).

Modern endoscopes use a **fiber-optic bundle** (coherent image guide) for illumination and image transmission, with a small **CCD/CMOS camera** at the distal tip. Procedures include:
- **Gastroscopy** (esophagus, stomach, duodenum)
- **Colonoscopy** (colon)
- **Bronchoscopy** (trachea, bronchi)
- **Laparoscopy** (abdominal cavity, through a small incision)
- **Cystoscopy** (urinary bladder)

**Connection to X-ray imaging:** Some endoscopic procedures (e.g., **ERCP — Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography**) combine endoscopy with **fluoroscopy** and **contrast injection** to visualize the biliary and pancreatic ducts. So endoscopy often interfaces with X-ray fluoroscopy.

## 2.7 Use 5: Diathermy (Electro-Surgery)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diathermy = therapeutic heating of body tissues using high-frequency alternating electric current.** It is *not* an X-ray application but is grouped with it in the KTU module because it is another modality of **radiological/electromagnetic energy used in medical intervention**.

**Types:**
1. **Short-wave Diathermy (SWD):** 27.12 MHz — deep tissue heating (muscles, joints).
2. **Microwave Diathermy (MWD):** 2,450 MHz — shallower heating.
3. **Surgical Diathermy (Electrocautery):** 0.3–5 MHz — used in operating rooms for **cutting** (continuous waveform) or **coagulation** (intermittent waveform) of tissue.

**Mechanism:** HF current causes rapid oscillation of tissue ions, leading to **resistive (Joule) heating**: $P = I^2 R$.

**Two electrode configurations:**
- **Monopolar** — active electrode at surgical site, large return (dispersive) electrode on patient's back/thigh.
- **Bipolar** — both electrodes at the surgical tip (e.g., bipolar forceps), current flows only through tissue grasped between the tips.

**Safety Caution:** Use of diathermy is **contra-indicated in patients with implanted pacemakers**, as HF interference can cause pacemaker malfunction. KTU examiners love this viva point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is the **board-exam workhorse**. Every derivation here is a frequently-asked 7-mark or 14-mark question in KTU ESE. Work through every line — examiners reward each correct intermediate step.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Duane–Hunt Law and Minimum Wavelength

**Problem statement:** Derive the expression for the **minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube** in terms of the accelerating voltage. State the numerical value of the constant and find $\lambda_{\min}$ for a tube operating at 100 kV.

**Given:** Tube potential $V$ (volts), electron charge $e$, Planck's constant $h$, speed of light $c$.

### Step 1 — Energy acquired by an electron in the field
An electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$ gains kinetic energy:

$$E_k = eV$$

This is the **definition of the electron-volt (eV)**. At 1 volt, an electron gains exactly $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.

### Step 2 — Maximum possible photon energy
When this electron strikes the target and is **completely stopped in a single collision** with the nucleus, the **entire kinetic energy** is released as **one photon** of energy $E_{\max}$:

$$E_{\max} = eV$$

This is the *maximum* possible photon energy — collisions releasing less energy produce lower-energy (longer-wavelength) photons. Hence $E_{\max}$ corresponds to $\lambda_{\min}$.

### Step 3 — Use the Planck–Einstein relation
Photon energy is also given by:

$$E = h f = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

Setting $E_{\max} = eV$ and $f_{\max} = c / \lambda_{\min}$:

$$eV = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda_{\min}$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{h c}{eV}$$

**Substituting constants:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^{8})}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V} = \frac{1.2406 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ m}$$

Or in the more memorable form used in KTU answers:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min}(\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{V(\text{volts})}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation for 100 kV
At $V = 100{,}000$ V:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1240}{100{,}000} = 0.0124 \text{ nm} = 1.24 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = 0.124 \text{ Å}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Interpretation:** A 100 kV tube produces X-rays with wavelengths as short as 0.0124 nm — far smaller than the diameter of an atom (~0.1 nm). This is why X-rays can pass between atoms and through the body.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: X-ray Attenuation (Beer–Lambert Law)

**Problem statement:** A monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a tissue slab of thickness $x$. The linear attenuation coefficient is $\mu$. Derive the transmitted intensity $I(x)$. Show that the **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** is $x_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \mu$.

### Step 1 — Set up the differential equation
Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of tissue of thickness $dx$. The fraction of photons absorbed (or scattered) in this slice is proportional to the slice thickness:

$$dI = -\mu I \, dx$$

The negative sign indicates that intensity *decreases* with $x$.

### Step 2 — Separate variables
Rearranging:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to $x$
Applying the boundary condition $I(0) = I_0$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\mu x$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $I$

$$\ln \left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right) = -\mu x$$

$$\boxed{\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x} \quad \text{or} \quad I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}}$$

This is the **Beer–Lambert law of X-ray attenuation**.

### Step 5 — Derive the Half-Value Layer
By definition, the HVL is the thickness $x_{1/2}$ that reduces the intensity to half its original value, i.e., $I = I_0 / 2$:

$$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x_{1/2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\mu x_{1/2}}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \frac{1}{2} = -\mu x_{1/2}$$

Since $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$:

$$-\ln 2 = -\mu x_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}}$$

### Step 6 — Verification with a numerical example
For muscle tissue at 50 keV, $\mu \approx 0.2$ cm⁻¹. Therefore:

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.2} = 3.47 \text{ cm}$$

A 3.47 cm thick slab of muscle halves the X-ray intensity — clinically, this matches observed chest X-ray behaviour.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Continuous X-ray Spectrum Shape (Kramers' Law)

**Statement:** The intensity of the continuous (Bremsstrahlung) X-ray spectrum is given by Kramers' law (1923):

$$I(\lambda) \, d\lambda = K \cdot i \cdot Z \cdot \left( \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_{\min}} - 1 \right) \frac{d\lambda}{\lambda^2}$$

where $K$ is a constant, $i$ is tube current, $Z$ is target atomic number, $\lambda_{\min}$ is the Duane–Hunt cut-off wavelength.

**Simplified conclusion** (used in KTU answers):
- Spectrum **starts at zero** at $\lambda = \lambda_{\min}$,
- **Rises linearly** to a peak at $\lambda \approx 1.5 \, \lambda_{\min}$,
- **Decays** as $1/\lambda^2$ at long wavelengths (subject to self-absorption within the target — the **Heel effect**).

**Key engineering take-aways from Kramers' law:**

| Parameter | Effect on Total X-ray Intensity | Engineering Lever |
|-----------|----------------------------------|-------------------|
| Tube current $i$ | Linear ($I \propto i$) | Adjust mA to control image density |
| Target atomic number $Z$ | Linear ($I \propto Z$) | Use tungsten (Z=74) instead of Cu (Z=29) for diagnostic tubes |
| Tube voltage $V$ | Quadratic ($I \propto V^2$ approximately) | Adjust kVp to control image contrast |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Five X-ray Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Mastery Matrix —** This is the kind of comparison table KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers. The bolded entries are most-asked.

| Property | Radiography | Fluoroscopy | Angiography (DSA) | Endoscopy | Diathermy |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Output type** | Static image | Real-time video | Static / dynamic vessel map | Direct visual image | Tissue heating / cut |
| **Energy source** | X-ray photons (30–120 kVp) | X-ray photons (60–120 kVp) | X-ray photons (60–100 kVp) | White light (fiber-optic) **or** X-ray (in ERCP) | HF current 0.3–27 MHz |
| **Detector** | Film / FPD / CR plate | Image Intensifier + CCD | FPD + digital subtractor | Eyepiece / CMOS at tip | Tissue is the "detector" (heat sink) |
| **Contrast medium** | Air / BaSO₄ / Iodine | Iodine (sometimes) | Iodine (mandatory) | Air / CO₂ / saline | None (electric current) |
| **Patient dose (typical)** | 0.02 mGy (chest) | 5–15 mGy/min | 10–50 mGy per run | Negligible ionizing dose | None (non-ionizing) |
| **Diagnostic / Therapeutic** | Diagnostic | Diagnostic / interventional | Diagnostic | Diagnostic | Therapeutic |
| **Real-time?** | No | Yes | Yes (if live mode) | Yes | Yes |
| **Risk of ionizing radiation?** | Yes (low) | Yes (high — cumulative) | Yes (high) | No (fiber-optic) | No |
| **Key clinical example** | Chest X-ray for TB | Barium swallow study | Coronary angiogram for CAD | Colonoscopy for polyps | Tissue cutting in OR |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — HVL Application

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Question (KTU-style):** The HVL of an aluminium filter for 80 keV X-rays is 4.0 mm. Calculate (a) the linear attenuation coefficient of aluminium, (b) the thickness of Al required to reduce the beam intensity to 1/8 of its original value.

**Solution:**

**(a)** From the HVL formula:
$$\mu = \frac{\ln 2}{x_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{0.004 \text{ m}} = 173.25 \text{ m}^{-1} = 1.73 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**(b)** We need $I / I_0 = 1/8 = 2^{-3}$. Since each HVL halves the intensity, **three HVLs** are required:
$$x = 3 \times x_{1/2} = 3 \times 4.0 \text{ mm} = 12.0 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \text{ cm}$$

Verification using the direct formula:
$$I = I_0 e^{-\mu x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{8} = e^{-173.25 \times 0.012}$$
$$e^{-2.079} = 0.125 \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coolidge X-ray Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hvt[High Voltage Transformer 30 to 150 kV]
    ft[Filament Transformer 3 to 5 A 10 V]
    filcath[Tungsten Filament Cathode]
    foc[Focusing Cup]
    vac[High Vacuum Envelope 10 to 7 Torr]
    rota[Rotating Tungsten Anode Z equals 74]
    bescr[Bremsstrahlung Continuous Spectrum]
    charr[Characteristic K alpha K beta Peaks]
    bew[Beryllium Window Z equals 4]
    pat[Patient Body]
    imd[Image Detector Film FPD II]
    ind[Induction Motor 3000 to 10000 rpm]
    sink[Anode Heat Exchanger Radiator]

    hvt -->|kVp potential| filcath
    hvt -->|kVp potential| rota
    ft -->|Heating current| filcath
    filcath -->|Thermionic electrons| foc
    foc -->|Focused electron beam| rota
    rota -->|Braking radiation| bescr
    rota -->|Inner shell ejection| charr
    bescr --> bew
    charr --> bew
    bew -->|Filtered X-ray beam| pat
    pat -->|Attenuated beam| imd
    ind -->|Drives rotation| rota
    rota -->|99 percent heat| sink
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Diagnostic X-ray Imaging Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hvg[High Voltage Generator] --> tube[X-ray Tube]
    tube --> coll[Collimator]
    coll -->|Cone beam| pat[Patient]
    pat -->|Attenuated beam| tab[Table Bucky Grid]
    tab -->|Anti scatter grid| det[Detector Film FPD II]
    det --> proc[Image Processor DSA]
    proc --> mon[Display Monitor]
    proc --> arch[PACS Archive]
    console[Operator Console] -->|Set kVp mA s| hvg
    console -->|Trigger exposure| tube
    console --> proc
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Image Intensifier (Fluoroscopy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    xr[X ray Photons from Patient] --> ip[Input Phosphor CsI]
    ip -->|Visible light photons| pc[Photocathode Sb Cs]
    pc -->|Electrons emitted| el[Electrostatic Electron Lens]
    el -->|Accelerated 25 kV| op[Output Phosphor ZnCdS]
    op -->|Bright visible image 5000x| cc[CCD CMOS Camera]
    cc -->|Video signal| mon[TV Monitor 30 fps]
    cc --> dig[Digital Frame Grabber]
    dig --> arch[Hard Disk Storage]
    dig --> mon
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Angiography (DSA) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pre[Acquire Mask Image Pre Contrast] --> reg[Register Images Align]
    inj[Inject Iodine Contrast via Catheter]
    inj -->|Bolus reaches ROI| dyn[Acquire Dynamic Series 5 to 10 fps]
    dyn --> sub[Digital Subtraction Mask minus Live]
    sub --> en[Contrast Enhancement Window Level]
    en --> rec[Recurse Mask Refresh Every 2 Frames]
    en --> dis[Display Angiogram on Monitor]
    en --> store[Store DICOM in PACS]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Surgical Diathermy (Electro-Surgery)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    osc[Oscillator 0.3 to 5 MHz Sine] --> amp[Power Amplifier]
    amp --> iso[Isolation Transformer Patient Safety]
    iso --> at[Active Electrode]
    at -->|High density current| tis[Tissue at Surgical Site]
    tis -->|Return current| ret[Return Electrode Dispersive Pad]
    ret --> iso
    at -.->|CUT mode continuous wave| cutmod[Tissue Vaporization]
    at -.->|COAG mode burst pulse| coagmod[Tissue Coagulation]
    foot[Foot Switch or Hand Piece] -->|Select mode| osc
    mon[Patient Return Electrode Monitor REM] -->|Impedance check 30 to 150 ohm| iso
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?**
> The KTU board examiner often awards 1–2 extra marks for a clean, labelled block diagram even when the answer is textually perfect. Mermaid renders correctly in the KTU digital answer sheet preview tool (when available) and conveys signal-flow understanding at a glance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**, and the expected answer length is approximately **80–120 words** with at least one formula or diagram. Answer **5 out of 6** in 30 minutes.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define Bremsstrahlung radiation. How does it differ from characteristic X-radiation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bremsstrahlung radiation** (German for "braking radiation") is the **continuous-spectrum** X-ray emission produced when a high-velocity electron is **decelerated and deflected** by the strong Coulomb field of a target nucleus. The energy lost by the electron appears as an X-ray photon of **any energy from zero up to the full kinetic energy** of the incident electron, yielding a continuous spectrum.

**Characteristic X-radiation**, in contrast, is a **discrete-line** emission produced when an incident electron **ejects an inner-shell (K-shell)** electron of a target atom, and an outer-shell electron drops down to fill the vacancy, emitting a photon whose energy equals the **difference between the two shell energies** (e.g., $K_\alpha$, $K_\beta$ lines). Its energy is **characteristic of the target material**.

The two spectra together — continuous Bremsstrahlung with superimposed sharp characteristic peaks — constitute the output of a typical diagnostic X-ray tube.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**List the differences between Radiography and Fluoroscopy. What is the role of the image intensifier?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Radiography | Fluoroscopy |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Output | Single static image | Real-time moving image (continuous) |
| Duration | Short (milliseconds) | Long (seconds to minutes) |
| Detector | X-ray film / FPD | Image intensifier + CCD camera |
| Patient dose | Low (≈0.02 mGy for chest) | High cumulative (5–15 mGy/min) |
| Use | Diagnostic snapshot | Dynamic studies (swallowing, catheter placement) |

The **image intensifier (II) tube** is the heart of a fluoroscopy system. It amplifies the faint X-ray image by a factor of **1,000–5,000×** through a four-stage process: (1) **input phosphor (CsI)** converts X-rays to visible light, (2) **photocathode** emits electrons, (3) **electrostatic lens** accelerates and focuses the electrons, and (4) **output phosphor** reconverts them to a much brighter, minified visible image captured by a **CCD/CMOS camera** and displayed on a monitor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Answer **2 out of 3** questions in 90 minutes. Both internal choices below are independent — the examiner must provide the candidate's chosen option only.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Coolidge X-ray tube**. Mention the role of the rotating anode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted** in terms of the accelerating voltage. An X-ray tube operates at 60 kV. Calculate the minimum wavelength produced. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a)

**Construction:** A Coolidge X-ray tube consists of the following components housed inside a **high-vacuum glass envelope** at $10^{-7}$ Torr:
1. **Cathode:** A tungsten filament (Z=74, melting point 3422 °C) wound into a small coil, surrounded by a **focusing cup (Wehnelt cylinder)** held at the same potential as the filament to focus the emitted electrons onto the anode focal spot.
2. **Anode:** A **rotating tungsten disc** (typically 75–120 mm diameter) bevelled at an angle of 7°–20° to direct the X-ray beam toward the patient window. Rhenium alloying (5–10 %) improves grain stability and thermal ruggedness.
3. **High-Voltage Supply:** 30–150 kV DC potential applied between cathode and anode.
4. **Beryllium Window:** Low-Z (Z=4) exit window allowing transmission of soft (low-energy) X-rays.
5. **Induction Motor:** External stator rotates the anode at 3,000–10,000 RPM via electromagnetic induction (no shaft through the vacuum).
6. **Heat Exchanger / Radiator:** Dissipates the ~99 % waste heat from the anode.

**Working:** A **low-voltage AC** (3–5 A at 10 V) heats the cathode filament to ~2,500 K, causing **thermionic emission** of electrons. The applied high DC potential (kVp) accelerates these electrons to a velocity approaching ½ the speed of light. On impact with the rotating tungsten target, the electrons undergo **two types of interactions**: (i) **Bremsstrahlung** (deceleration in the nuclear Coulomb field → continuous spectrum) and (ii) **Characteristic radiation** (K-shell electron ejection → discrete lines). The X-rays exit through the Be window, are **collimated** into a useful beam, and directed at the patient.

**Role of Rotating Anode:** The focal spot (0.1–2 mm) concentrates a huge power density (~10⁶ W/mm²) on a small area. A stationary anode would melt. By rotating at high RPM, the heat is **distributed over a large annular focal track** (~100 cm² effective area), increasing the **thermal load capacity by 10–20×** and permitting higher tube currents, shorter exposures, and finer focal spots for better resolution.

**Block diagram:** See §4.1 of these notes.

#### Solution to A(b)

**Derivation:** As derived in §3.1 of these notes, the minimum wavelength is given by:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{h c}{e V}$$

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.2406 \times 10^{-6}}{V(\text{volts})} \text{ m}$$

For $V = 60 \text{ kV} = 60{,}000$ V:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.2406 \times 10^{-6}}{60{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min} = 2.0677 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} \approx 0.0207 \text{ nm} = 0.207 \text{ Å}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — A(b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kV to V** (writing 60 instead of 60,000) — loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Leaving the answer in metres** without converting to nm/Å — examiners in KTU expect a clinically intuitive unit; 0.0207 nm is more meaningful than $2.07 \times 10^{-11}$ m.
> 3. **Not stating the Duane–Hunt law by name** — costs 1 mark; the law is a named, examinable topic.
> 4. Skipping the formula-substitution step is the **#1 cause of losing the "method" mark** (3 of 7 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **X-ray fluoroscopy** with a block diagram. List the components of an **image intensifier tube** and explain its function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Angiography**? Explain the **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** technique with a flowchart. State two **contrast media** used and explain why iodine is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a)

**Principle of Fluoroscopy:** Fluoroscopy provides **real-time, continuous X-ray imaging** of moving internal structures (e.g., heart beat, swallowing, GI tract motion, catheter navigation). A continuous or pulsed low-dose X-ray beam is transmitted through the patient; the transmitted beam carries the dynamic shadow information of internal organs, which is amplified, captured, and displayed on a monitor at typically 25–30 frames per second.

**Block diagram:** See §4.3 of these notes (Mermaid block diagram of the II chain).

**Components and function of the Image Intensifier tube:**

| # | Component | Material | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Input Window** | Aluminium or titanium (~0.5 mm) | Mechanical support, vacuum seal |
| 2 | **Input Phosphor (Screen)** | CsI (Cesium Iodide) | Converts incident X-ray photons to visible-light photons (efficiency ~60 %) |
| 3 | **Photocathode** | Sb-Cs (Antimony-Cesium) | Converts visible-light photons to electrons via the photoelectric effect |
| 4 | **Electrostatic Lens** | Electrodes at 25–30 kV | Accelerates and focuses the photoelectrons, **demagnifies** image from 25–40 cm input diameter to 2.5–5 cm output diameter (brightness gain ~100×) |
| 5 | **Output Phosphor** | ZnCdS (Zinc Cadmium Sulphide), green-emitting | Converts the accelerated, focused electron image back to a much **brighter visible image** (~5,000× brighter than input image) |
| 6 | **Output Window / Fibre-optic** | Glass | Couples the bright image to a CCD/CMOS camera |
| 7 | **Vacuum Envelope** | Glass / metal-ceramic | Maintains high vacuum (~10⁻⁶ Torr) |
| 8 | **Power Supply** | HV DC | Provides accelerating voltage to the photocathode and electron optics |

**Two-stage brightness gain:**
- **Flux gain** (from acceleration): ~50×
- **Minification gain** (from demagnification): ~100×
- **Total brightness gain:** ~5,000×

This amplification is essential because the unamplified fluoroscopic image is too dim to be seen by the human eye.

#### Solution to B(b)

**Angiography** is the **radiological imaging of blood vessels** after the introduction of a **radiopaque contrast medium** (typically iodine-based). It is used to diagnose **stenosis, occlusion, aneurysm, malformations**, and to guide **interventional procedures** such as angioplasty and stenting.

**Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) — Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **Pre-contrast "Mask" acquisition:** A plain X-ray image of the region of interest (ROI) is acquired *before* the contrast is injected. This "mask" contains all the overlying structures (bones, soft tissue).
2. **Contrast injection:** An iodinated contrast medium is injected intra-arterially (typically via a catheter in the femoral or radial artery) at a rate of 10–25 mL/s.
3. **Dynamic image acquisition:** As the contrast bolus flows through the ROI, a rapid series of images (5–10 fps) is acquired.
4. **Image registration:** Slight patient motion between mask and live images is corrected by **2-D image registration** algorithms.
5. **Pixel-by-pixel subtraction:** The mask image is **numerically subtracted** from each live image: $I_{\text{DSA}}(x,y) = I_{\text{live}}(x,y) - I_{\text{mask}}(x,y)$.
6. **Result:** All static background (bone, soft tissue) is **cancelled to zero**, leaving **only the contrast-filled vessels** visible.
7. **Display and storage:** The DSA images are displayed on a monitor, archived in PACS, and reviewed for diagnosis.

**Flowchart:** See §4.4 of these notes.

**Two contrast media:**
- **Iohexol (Omnipaque)** — non-ionic, low-osmolality.
- **Iopamidol (Isovue)** — non-ionic, water-soluble.

**Why iodine is preferred:**
1. **High atomic number (Z = 53)** → strong photoelectric absorption at diagnostic X-ray energies.
2. **K-edge at 33.2 keV**, which is **within the diagnostic X-ray energy range** (20–100 keV) → maximum photoelectric interaction.
3. **Non-toxic, water-soluble, and rapidly excreted** by the kidneys.
4. Forms **stable, injectable compounds** with low allergenic potential (especially non-ionic forms).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — B(b):**
> 1. **Confusing "contrast medium" with "contrast agent resolution"** — examiners deduct 2 marks if the student doesn't explain *why* iodine has high contrast (mention **K-edge at 33.2 keV** — this is the magic phrase).
> 2. **Skipping the actual subtraction equation** $I_{\text{DSA}} = I_{\text{live}} - I_{\text{mask}}$ — worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the typical injection rate** (mL/s) — shows practical knowledge; worth ½ mark.
> 4. **Forgetting patient-safety warning** — non-ionic iodine contrast carries a small risk of nephrotoxicity and allergic reaction. Mentioning this earns a "good clinical understanding" mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin It.**

- [x] **X-rays** = **ionizing EM radiation**, wavelength **0.01–10 nm**, photon energy **100 eV–100 keV**. Discovered by **Röntgen (1895)**.
- [x] **X-ray tube (Coolidge)** has a **tungsten filament cathode** and a **rotating tungsten anode** in a high vacuum ($10^{-7}$ Torr).
- [x] **Four stages of generation:** Thermionic emission → Acceleration → Sudden deceleration at anode → X-ray emission through Be window.
- [x] **Bremsstrahlung** = continuous spectrum (deceleration); **Characteristic** = discrete lines (inner-shell e⁻ ejection).
- [x] **Duane–Hunt law:** $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{h c}{e V} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ nm·V}}{V(\text{V})}$ — memorize this formula.
- [x] **Beer–Lambert attenuation law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — memorize this formula.
- [x] **Half-Value Layer (HVL):** $x_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ — every HVL halves the intensity.
