$$
\boxed{HCF = \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{10\ \mu\text{A}} = 10{,}000}
$$

The factor of **10,000** means the heart is 10,000 times more sensitive when current is applied directly to it. This single number is the strongest mathematical justification for the strict isolation of all patient-connected circuits (e.g., ECG input amplifiers, pacemaker leads, central venous catheters).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Pathways of Current Through a Patient in a Hospital Bed

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the various conduction paths (both normal and fault) through which leakage or fault current can reach a patient. It also maps the engineered safeguards that interrupt each path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mains["MAINS 230V 50Hz LIVE"]:::source
    Neutral["MAINS NEUTRAL"]:::neutral
    Earth["EARTH GROUND BUS"]:::earth

    Dev1["ECG MONITOR CLASS I"]:::device
    Dev2["DEFIBRILLATOR CLASS II"]:::device
    Dev3["INFUSION PUMP CLASS I"]:::device

    ChassisA["CHASSIS A"]:::chassis
    ChassisB["CHASSIS B"]:::chassis
    ChassisC["CHASSIS C"]:::chassis

    Patient["PATIENT BODY"]:::patient
    Heart["HEART MICROSHOCK ZONE"]:::heart
    Skin["SKIN CONTACT POINT"]:::skin

    GFCI["GFCI 5mA TRIP 25ms"]:::protector
    EquipGround["EQUIPOTENTIAL GROUND BUS"]:::protector
    IsoAmp["ISOLATION AMPLIFIER"]:::protector
    DoubInsul["DOUBLE INSULATION BARRIER"]:::protector

    Mains --> Dev1
    Mains --> Dev2
    Mains --> Dev3
    Neutral --> Dev1
    Neutral --> Dev2
    Neutral --> Dev3

    Dev1 --> ChassisA
    Dev2 --> ChassisB
    Dev3 --> ChassisC

    ChassisA -.->|"LEAKAGE 500uA"| Earth
    ChassisB -.->|"DOUBLE INSULATION NO LEAK"| ChassisB
    ChassisC -.->|"LEAKAGE 800uA"| Earth

    ChassisA -->|"FAULT TOUCH MACROSHOCK"| Skin
    ChassisC -->|"FAULT TOUCH MACROSHOCK"| Skin
    Skin --> Patient
    Patient --> Heart

    Dev1 -->|"ECG LEAD COUPLED"| IsoAmp
    IsoAmp -->|"ISOLATED SIGNAL ONLY"| Heart

    Mains --> GFCI
    GFCI --> Dev1
    GFCI --> Dev3

    ChassisA --> EquipGround
    ChassisC --> EquipGround
    Patient --> EquipGround

    classDef source fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef neutral fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
    classDef earth fill:#8d6e63,stroke:#3e2723,color:#fff
    classDef device fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef chassis fill:#90a4ae,stroke:#263238,color:#fff
    classDef patient fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef heart fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff
    classDef skin fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef protector fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are intended current paths (mains powering devices, signal from ECG lead). **Dotted arrows are leakage / fault paths.** Green nodes are **protective devices** (GFCI, isolation amplifier, equipotential bus, double insulation). The diagram visually proves that even with one protective barrier, redundant layers are needed for patient safety.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Medical Equipment Protection Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ClassI["CLASS I EQUIPMENT"]:::c1
    ClassII["CLASS II EQUIPMENT"]:::c2
    ClassIII["CLASS III EQUIPMENT"]:::c3
    IP["INTERNALLY POWERED"]:::ip

    ClassI -->|"REQUIRES"| PE["PROTECTIVE EARTH WIRE"]
    ClassII -->|"HAS"| DI["DOUBLE INSULATION REINFORCED"]
    ClassIII -->|"USES"| SELV["SELV BATTERY BELOW 25V AC 60V DC"]
    IP -->|"HAS"| IB["INTERNAL BATTERY ONLY"]

    PE --> Result["SAFETY FROM EARTH BOND"]
    DI --> Result
    SELV --> Result
    IB --> Result

    classDef c1 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff
    classDef c2 fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef c3 fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    classDef ip fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Method of Accident Prevention — Multi-Layer Defense

The defense is structured in **four concentric layers**. Failure of any single layer is compensated by the next. This is called the **"Swiss Cheese Model"** of safety engineering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LAYER 1 PRIMARY PREVENTION"]:::l1
    L2["LAYER 2 ENGINEERED DESIGN"]:::l2
    L3["LAYER 3 PROTECTIVE DEVICES"]:::l3
    L4["LAYER 4 MONITORING AND PROCEDURE"]:::l4

    L1 -->|"INCLUDES"| L1a["PROPER INSTALLATION"]:::detail
    L1 -->|"INCLUDES"| L1b["STAFF TRAINING"]:::detail
    L1 -->|"INCLUDES"| L1c["PERIODIC INSPECTION"]:::detail

    L2 -->|"INCLUDES"| L2a["DOUBLE INSULATION CLASS II"]:::detail
    L2 -->|"INCLUDES"| L2b["LOW VOLTAGE DESIGN"]:::detail
    L2 -->|"INCLUDES"| L2c["INTRINSIC SAFETY"]:::detail

    L3 -->|"INCLUDES"| L3a["GFCI 5mA"]:::detail
    L3 -->|"INCLUDES"| L3b["ISOLATION TRANSFORMERS"]:::detail
    L3 -->|"INCLUDES"| L3c["EQUIPOTENTIAL GROUNDING"]:::detail

    L4 -->|"INCLUDES"| L4a["LEAKAGE CURRENT TESTS"]:::detail
    L4 -->|"INCLUDES"| L4b["ALARM SYSTEMS"]:::detail
    L4 -->|"INCLUDES"| L4c["MAINTENANCE PROTOCOLS"]:::detail

    Fault["ELECTRICAL FAULT"]:::fault --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> Safe["PATIENT PROTECTED"]:::safe

    classDef l1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef l2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef l3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef l4 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#ffffff,stroke:#666,color:#000
    classDef fault fill:#ff5252,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff
    classDef safe fill:#66bb6a,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain methods of accident prevention," structure your answer along these **four layers**. Examiners reward systematic answers that move from *organizational* → *design* → *device* → *monitoring* methods.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define microshock. Why is it more dangerous than macroshock even though the current is 10,000 times smaller?**

**Model Answer:**

**Microshock** is the passage of a very small electrical current (of the order of **microamperes, typically 10 µA at 50/60 Hz**) directly through the myocardium (heart muscle) via an internal conductive path such as a catheter, pacemaker lead, or saline-filled central line.

It is more dangerous than macroshock because:

1. **Current bypasses the skin** — the skin's high resistance (~10 kΩ dry, ~1 kΩ wet) is the body's main protective barrier. Microshock delivers current through a conductor that *bypasses* this barrier entirely, so even a tiny voltage can drive a lethal current through the heart.
2. **Current density at the heart is extremely high** — concentrated at a small cross-sectional area of cardiac tissue.
3. **Patient is unaware** — the small current does not cause pain or muscle contraction (no warning sign), unlike macroshock where the victim jerks away.
4. **Heart Current Factor (HCF) of 10,000** — the heart is 10,000 times more sensitive to direct current than to skin-applied current. So while macroshock fibrillation requires ~100 mA, microshock fibrillation occurs at ~10 µA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often answer "because microshock current is smaller, it's less dangerous." This is the **exact opposite** of the truth. Microshock is dangerous *because* the same physiological damage is achieved with 10,000× less current. Always emphasize **HCF = 10,000** in your answer.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**What is a Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI)? State its typical trip current and trip time.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI)** is a fast-acting protective device that monitors the current balance between the live and neutral conductors of a circuit. Under normal operation, the current in the live wire equals the current returning through the neutral wire. If even a small difference (typically **5 mA** for medical / personnel-protection GFCIs) is detected, it means current is leaking to ground through an unintended path — possibly through a human body. The GFCI disconnects the supply in **less than 25 milliseconds**, well before ventricular fibrillation can be triggered.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Trip current (medical GFCI) | **5 mA** |
| Maximum trip time | **≤ 25 ms** |
| Working principle | Differential current comparison (live vs. neutral) |
| Standard reference | IEC 61008 / NFPA 99 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Module Choice: Either Question A OR Question B

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **physiological effects of electric current** on the human body. With the help of a current-versus-effect curve, list the major threshold currents and their consequences for a 50 Hz AC supply. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **macroshock and microshock** with suitable examples. Why is microshock a critical concern in the ICU and cardiac catheterization lab? Explain the concept of the **Heart Current Factor (HCF)** and compute its value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**
The severity of an electric shock depends not on voltage but on the **magnitude, frequency, duration, and pathway** of the current that actually flows through the body. The human body is a frequency-dependent impedance, and at 50 Hz, the current is the primary determinant of physiological damage.

**Step 2: Threshold Table (3 marks)**

| Threshold | Current (50 Hz AC) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Perception | 1 mA | Faint tingling |
| Let-go (men) | 10 mA | Cannot release conductor |
| Let-go (women) | 6 mA | Cannot release conductor |
| Respiratory paralysis | 20–50 mA | Diaphragm locks |
| Pain / severe fatigue | 50–100 mA | Intense muscle contraction |
| Ventricular fibrillation | ≥ 100 mA (for > 1 s) | Lethal arrhythmia |

**Step 3: Current-Duration Relationship (2 marks)**
According to IEC 60479, the product $I \cdot t$ is roughly constant for fibrillation onset:

$$
I \cdot t \approx 30\ \text{mA·s} \quad \text{(empirical fibrillation limit)}
$$

So a 100 mA current fibrillates in ~0.3 s, while a 1 A current fibrillates in ~30 ms.

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**
For 50 Hz AC, the **1 mA / 10 mA / 100 mA** rule of thumb is the most important takeaway. Hospital-grade protection devices are calibrated to interrupt current well before the 10 mA let-go threshold.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definitions (2 marks)**
- **Macroshock:** Electrical current applied to the body through the **external skin surface**. Typical magnitude: mA range. Patient feels pain, muscle contraction, and can usually withdraw.
- **Microshock:** Electrical current applied **directly to the heart** via an internal conductor (catheter, pacemaker lead, saline-filled line). Typical magnitude: µA range. Patient feels **nothing**.

**Step 2: Comparison Table (2 marks)**

| Feature | Macroshock | Microshock |
|---|---|---|
| Magnitude | mA | µA |
| Entry point | Skin | Direct cardiac contact |
| Patient awareness | Yes | **None** |
| Fatal threshold | 100 mA | 10 µA |
| Typical site | General ward, OR | ICU, CCU, cath lab |

**Step 3: Heart Current Factor Derivation (2 marks)**

$$
HCF = \frac{I_{body, fibrillation}}{I_{heart, fibrillation}} = \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{10\ \mu\text{A}}
$$

$$
HCF = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-1}}{10^{-5}} = 10^4 = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 4: ICU / Cath Lab Relevance (1 mark)**
In the ICU, patients often have multiple intravascular lines, indwelling catheters, and pacemaker leads. Even **10 µA** of leakage current from a faulty infusion pump or bedside monitor can travel directly to the heart and cause fibrillation. This is why **isolated patient circuits, GFCIs, and equipotential grounding** are mandatory in these zones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In part (b), students frequently forget to actually **compute the HCF value of 10,000**. Just defining it is worth only ~1 mark. The numerical calculation and unit conversion (mA → A, µA → A) carry the remaining marks. Always show: $I_{heart} = 10\ \mu\text{A} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What are the **shock hazards arising from medical electrical equipment**? Classify medical equipment into Class I, Class II, and Class III, and explain how each class provides protection against electric shock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **methods of accident prevention** in a hospital environment, with specific reference to grounding, isolation, GFCI, double insulation, and equipotential bonding. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Types of Shock Hazards (2 marks)**
- **Direct contact** with live conductors (broken insulation, exposed wires).
- **Indirect contact** with metal chassis that has become energized due to insulation failure.
- **Leakage current** paths (capacitive coupling, defective insulation).
- **Microshock** via patient-connected conductors.

**Step 2: Equipment Classification (3 marks)**

| Class | Description | Protection Method | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Has accessible metal parts that **must** be bonded to earth via a protective earth wire. | Basic insulation + protective earth (PE) — if insulation fails, fault current flows to earth and trips the breaker. | X-ray, ECG, large monitors |
| **Class II** | No protective earth needed; uses **double or reinforced insulation**. | Two independent insulation layers — if one fails, the second one holds. Marked with a "double square" symbol. | Infusion pumps, handheld devices, surgical tools |
| **Class III** | Powered by **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV)** from a battery or isolated source (< 25 V AC, < 60 V DC). | Voltage itself is below hazardous level. | Battery-operated pulse oximeters, thermometers |

**Step 3: Patient-Connected vs. Non-Patient-Connected (2 marks)**
- **Type B (Body):** Applied to body externally, provides adequate protection against macroshock, may have a grounded patient connection.
- **Type BF (Body Floating):** Has an isolated (floating) patient connection; provides protection against microshock.
- **Type CF (Cardiac Floating):** Highest isolation, suitable for direct cardiac application. **Mandatory in cath lab.**

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Method 1 — Protective Grounding (Earthing) (1.5 marks)**
Every Class I device's metal chassis is bonded to earth via a low-resistance conductor. If a live wire accidentally contacts the chassis, the fault current flows to earth, the protective fuse/breaker trips, and the chassis is de-energized. Resistance of protective earth < 0.1 Ω as per IEC 60601-1.

**Method 2 — Double Insulation (1 mark)**
Used in Class II equipment. Two independent insulation layers (basic + supplementary) ensure that even if one layer fails, the user is not exposed. Eliminates the need for an earth wire. Identified by the "double square" symbol ⬛⬛.

**Method 3 — Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI) (1.5 marks)**
A differential current sensor that compares live and neutral currents. Any imbalance > 5 mA triggers disconnection within 25 ms. Used in operating rooms, ICU, and wet locations (bathrooms, hydrotherapy).

**Method 4 — Isolation Transformers and Isolated Patient Circuits (1.5 marks)**
A medical-grade isolation transformer with high galvanic isolation (> 5 kV) between primary and secondary, often used with an **isolation line monitor (ILM)** that alarms if leakage exceeds a threshold. ECG input amplifiers use **isolation amplifiers** with > 5 kV isolation to break any direct galvanic path between mains and the patient.

**Method 5 — Equipotential Bonding (1 mark)**
In the operating room and ICU, all metal surfaces (chassis, bed rails, operating table) are connected to a single equipotential ground bus. This ensures no two surfaces can be at different potentials, so touching two surfaces simultaneously does not create a current path through the patient.

**Method 6 — Standards and Periodic Testing (0.5 mark)**
Adherence to **IEC 60601-1** for medical electrical equipment and **NFPA 99** for healthcare facilities, combined with periodic leakage current tests, insulation tests, and ground continuity tests, completes the safety framework.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In part (b), students often describe only **earthing** and forget to mention the equally important **isolation amplifier** used in patient-connected devices. Also, many confuse **equipotential bonding** (bonding all metal surfaces to the *same* ground reference) with **protective earthing** (bonding to earth). These are distinct concepts; mixing them up will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1-10-100 Rule (Most-Tested Fact):**
> - **1 mA** → perception threshold (50 Hz AC)
> - **10 mA** → let-go threshold (men, AC)
> - **100 mA for 1 s** → ventricular fibrillation

- **Body impedance** is frequency-dependent: dry skin ~100 kΩ, wet ~1 kΩ, internal ~500 Ω. At 50 Hz, current flows more easily than at DC.
- **Microshock** is 10,000× more dangerous than macroshock per unit current. **HCF = 10,000.**
- **Microshock lethal limit:** **10 µA** at 50/60 Hz applied directly to the heart.
- **Macroshock lethal limit:** **100 mA for > 1 s** (or equivalently, ~30 mA·s charge per phase).
- **Class I** = Basic insulation + protective earth (PE). **Class II** = Double/reinforced insulation (no PE). **Class III** = SELV (< 25 V AC / 60 V DC).
- **Patient-connected types:** Type B (basic), Type BF (body floating, isolated), Type CF (cardiac floating, highest safety).
- **GFCI** trips at 5 mA in ≤ 25 ms. **Earth leakage current limit** = 500 µA (normal), 5 mA (single-fault).
- **Defibrillator protection:** All medical equipment connected to a patient must withstand the high-voltage spikes from a defibrillator (typically 5 kV) without conducting dangerous current to the patient.
- **Indian mains** is 230 V, 50 Hz — sitting in the most physiologically dangerous band (40–100 Hz).
- **Standard:** IEC 60601-1 (global), IS 13450 (Indian), NFPA 99 (US, referenced).
- **Defense-in-depth:** Accident prevention is layered — Primary (training, installation) + Engineered (Class II, low voltage) + Devices (GFCI, isolation) + Monitoring (leakage tests, alarms).
- **Equipotential bonding ≠ Earthing** — Equipotential bonding ties all metal surfaces to the *same* potential, preventing inter-surface current flow.
- **In an answer, always include units and the numerical value** (e.g., "10 mA let-go threshold," "HCF = 10,000," "GFCI trips at 5 mA in 25 ms") — these are the most-missed scoring opportunities in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Telemedicine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT742 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Telemedicine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telemedicine** is the delivery of healthcare services, where distance is a critical factor, by healthcare professionals using information and communication technologies (ICT) for the exchange of valid information for the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of disease and injuries, research and evaluation, and for the continuing education of healthcare providers, all in the interests of advancing the health of individuals and their communities.

*(Source: World Health Organization - WHO, 1997 Definition)*

**Key Terms in the Definition:**
- **Healthcare Professionals**: Doctors, nurses, specialists, technicians
- **ICT (Information and Communication Technologies)**: Internet, satellites, mobile networks, computers
- **Valid Information**: Clinically relevant, accurate patient data (text, images, audio, video)
- **Distance Factor**: The patient and provider are geographically separated

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy: The "Virtual Hospital Visit"**
>
> Imagine you are sick in a remote village in Wayanad (Kerala), and the best cardiologist is in Kottayam. Without telemedicine, you would have to travel **~150 km**, losing a day of work, paying for transport, and waiting in long queues. With **telemedicine**, the doctor can see you through a video call, view your ECG report sent digitally, hear your heartbeat through a digital stethoscope connected to the internet, prescribe medicine, and print a digital prescription — all while you sit in your local Primary Health Centre (PHC).
>
> In essence, **Telemedicine = Healthcare + Internet + Distance Communication**.

## 1.3 Telemedicine vs. Telehealth — A Critical Distinction

| Feature | Telemedicine | Telehealth |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Narrow (clinical/curative) | Broad (clinical + non-clinical) |
| **Focus** | Diagnosis & treatment of patients | Includes education, awareness, public health, administration |
| **Provider** | Only licensed clinicians | Includes health educators, administrators |
| **Example** | Remote ECG consultation | Online health awareness webinar |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When a 2-mark question asks "Define telemedicine," students often write the broader *Telehealth* definition and lose marks. Use the **WHO 1997** definition strictly for telemedicine.

## 1.4 Brief History & Evolution

- **1924**: First tele-stethoscope demonstrated (Radio News magazine)
- **1950s–60s**: NASA transmits astronauts' physiological data to Earth (precursor of modern telemedicine)
- **1970s**: Telemedicine projects in remote Alaska and Canada
- **1990s**: Internet revolution enables wider adoption
- **2000s**: DICOM, HL7 standards formalized
- **2020 (COVID-19)**: Massive global adoption surge; India launches **eSanjeevani** national telemedicine platform
- **2024+**: AI-assisted remote diagnostics, 5G-enabled robotic surgery

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

- **Typical Latency Requirement:** < **200 ms** for real-time consultation
- **Minimum Bandwidth for Video Consultation:** **384 kbps**
- **DICOM Image Size (Chest X-ray):** ~**10–25 MB**
- **HL7 Messaging Standard:** Version **2.x** (most widely deployed)
- **eSanjeevani (India):** > **10 crore** teleconsultations as of 2023

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operational Steps of Telemedicine

The telemedicine workflow can be broken down into **five sequential logic steps**:

1. **Patient Data Acquisition**
   - At the patient site, biomedical instruments (ECG, BP monitor, pulse oximeter, digital stethoscope, glucometer) collect physiological signals.
   - These analog signals are converted to **digital form** using ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter).

2. **Data Encoding & Transmission**
   - Digital data is compressed (e.g., JPEG for images, MPEG-4 for video) to reduce bandwidth.
   - Encrypted (using protocols like **TLS 1.3 / AES-256**) to ensure **HIPAA / DISHA (India's Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act)** compliance.

3. **Network Transport**
   - Data travels via **PSTN, ISDN, broadband internet, 3G/4G/5G mobile networks, satellite links, or Wi-Fi**.
   - Routed through gateways/servers to the specialist's location.

4. **Specialist Review & Decision**
   - The clinician receives, decrypts, and views the data.
   - Diagnostic decision is made based on the transmitted information.

5. **Feedback & Prescription**
   - Diagnosis, prescription, and advice are transmitted back to the patient site.
   - Stored in **Electronic Health Record (EHR)** for future reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each step matters:** Skipping encryption (Step 2) violates patient privacy. Skipping compression overwhelms low-bandwidth rural networks. Skipping proper EHR storage breaks the continuity of care.

## 2.2 Classification of Telemedicine

### A. Based on **Timing of Information Exchange**

| Type | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Store-and-Forward (Asynchronous)** | Patient data is stored, then forwarded to specialist later | Sending a radiology image (X-ray, MRI) for later review |
| **Real-Time (Synchronous)** | Live interaction between patient and doctor | Video consultation over Zoom/eSanjeevani |
| **Remote Monitoring** | Continuous data streaming from patient's wearable/home device | Remote ICU monitoring, wearable ECG patches |

### B. Based on **Medical Specialty**

- **Teleradiology** — Transmission of radiological images
- **Telecardiology** — ECG, echocardiogram transmission
- **Telepathology** — Microscopic image transmission
- **Teledermatology** — Skin lesion images
- **Telepsychiatry** — Mental health video sessions
- **Tele-ICU** — Critical care from a distance

## 2.3 Core Components (Pillars) of Telemedicine

A telemedicine system requires **three integrated pillars**:

1. **Technology (Hardware + Software)**
   - Biomedical sensors, computers, servers, network equipment
   - Software: EHR systems, DICOM viewers, encryption tools

2. **People (Human Resources)**
   - Doctors, nurses, technicians at both ends
   - Trained IT support staff

3. **Process (Protocols & Policies)**
   - Legal framework, licensing, reimbursement
   - Standard operating procedures (SOPs), ethical guidelines

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although telemedicine is largely conceptual, the following **engineering formulas** are commonly tested in KTU exams.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Rate (R)} &= \text{Sampling Rate (f_s)} \times \text{Bits per Sample (n)} \times \text{Number of Channels (N)} \\[4pt]
\text{Compression Ratio (CR)} &= \frac{\text{Original File Size}}{\text{Compressed File Size}} \\[4pt]
\text{Storage Required (S)} &= \text{R} \times \text{Duration (T)} \quad \text{[in bits]} \\[4pt]
\text{Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)}_{dB} &= 6.02n + 1.76 \\[4pt]
\text{Nyquist Sampling Rate} &= 2 \times f_{max} \\[4pt]
\text{Transmission Time (T_t)} &= \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Bandwidth (B)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value in Telemedicine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling Rate (ECG) | $f_s$ | Hz (samples/sec) | **250–500 Hz** |
| Bits per Sample | $n$ | bits | **8, 12, 16** |
| Bandwidth (Video Consult) | $B$ | kbps / Mbps | **384 kbps – 2 Mbps** |
| Compression Ratio (JPEG) | $CR$ | dimensionless | **10:1 to 20:1** |
| Latency (Real-time) | $L$ | ms (milliseconds) | **< 200 ms** |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exam answers, **always show units** alongside numerical values. Mark deductions are common for missing units.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Healthcare Access:** Connects Kerala's tribal hamlets (Attappady) to super-specialty hospitals.
- **Disaster Response:** Triage patients in flood-affected areas (e.g., 2018 Kerala floods).
- **Chronic Disease Management:** Continuous glucose monitoring for diabetic patients.
- **Military & Space Medicine:** NASA's **Integrated Medical Model (IMM)** uses telemedicine principles for astronaut health.
- **Medical Education:** Live surgery broadcasts to medical colleges worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Problems & Solutions

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Introduction to Telemedicine" is largely conceptual, the following **analytical numerical problems** are the most commonly asked in KTU Board Exams based on this topic. They test your understanding of **data acquisition, compression, storage, and transmission** — the engineering backbone of telemedicine.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1: ECG Data Storage Calculation

**Problem:** A 12-lead ECG system samples each channel at **500 Hz** with **12-bit resolution**. The recording lasts **30 seconds**. Calculate:
(a) Total data generated (in MB)
(b) Storage required after 10:1 compression
(c) Transmission time over a **1 Mbps** internet connection (post-compression)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Sampling rate: $f_s = 500$ samples/sec/channel
- Number of channels (leads): $N = 12$
- Resolution (bits per sample): $n = 12$ bits
- Recording duration: $T = 30$ seconds
- Compression ratio: $CR = 10$
- Bandwidth: $B = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bits/sec

**Step 1: Calculate Total Data Rate (R)**

The data rate is the amount of data generated per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= f_s \times n \times N \\[4pt]
R &= 500 \times 12 \times 12 \\[4pt]
R &= 72{,}000 \text{ bits/second} \\[4pt]
R &= 72 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Raw Data (Step a)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Raw Data} &= R \times T \\[4pt]
&= 72{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \times 30 \text{ sec} \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}160{,}000 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2{,}160{,}000}{8 \times 10^6} \text{ MB} \\[4pt]
&= 0.27 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Compressed Data (Step b)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed Size} &= \frac{\text{Original Size}}{CR} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.27 \text{ MB}}{10} \\[4pt]
&= 0.027 \text{ MB} \; (\approx 27 \text{ KB})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Transmission Time (Step c)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed Size in bits} &= 0.027 \text{ MB} \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/MB} \\[4pt]
&= 216{,}000 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
\text{Transmission Time} &= \frac{216{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/sec}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.216 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (a) **0.27 MB** of raw ECG data
- (b) **0.027 MB** after compression
- (c) **0.216 seconds** transmission time over 1 Mbps

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Nyquist Sampling for Telemedicine Signals

**Problem:** A telemedicine system transmits a phonocardiogram (heart sound signal) with maximum frequency of **800 Hz**. Determine:
(a) Minimum Nyquist sampling rate
(b) If the system samples at **1.5 kHz** with 8-bit resolution for **1 channel**, calculate the data rate.
(c) How much storage is needed for a **5-minute** session?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:** $f_{max} = 800$ Hz, $f_s = 1500$ Hz, $n = 8$ bits, $N = 1$, $T = 5$ min $= 300$ sec

**Step 1: Apply Nyquist Theorem (Step a)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{s, \min} &= 2 \times f_{max} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 800 \\[4pt]
&= 1600 \text{ Hz} \; (\text{or } 1.6 \text{ kHz})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The chosen $f_s = 1500$ Hz is **less than** the Nyquist rate (1600 Hz). This would cause **aliasing**. The system should sample at $\geq 1600$ Hz.

**Step 2: Calculate Data Rate (Step b)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= f_s \times n \times N \\[4pt]
R &= 1500 \times 8 \times 1 \\[4pt]
R &= 12{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \\[4pt]
R &= 12 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Storage (Step c)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage} &= R \times T \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}000 \times 300 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}600{,}000 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{8 \times 10^6} \text{ MB} \\[4pt]
&= 0.45 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** (a) **1600 Hz**, (b) **12 kbps**, (c) **0.45 MB**

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Bandwidth & Transmission Time for DICOM Image

**Problem:** A chest X-ray is **25 MB** in size. A radiologist needs to download it over a **512 kbps** connection. Calculate the transmission time. If compressed with **JPEG 15:1**, what is the new transmission time? (Assume 1 MB = 1024 KB)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert units**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File Size} &= 25 \text{ MB} = 25 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
&= 209{,}715{,}200 \text{ bits} \approx 2.097 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Uncompressed Transmission Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{t1} &= \frac{2.097 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}}{512 \times 10^3 \text{ bits/sec}} \\[4pt]
&= 409.6 \text{ seconds} \approx 6.83 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Compressed File Size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed Size} &= \frac{25 \text{ MB}}{15} = 1.667 \text{ MB} \\[4pt]
\text{In bits} &= 1.667 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.398 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Compressed Transmission Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{t2} &= \frac{1.398 \times 10^7}{512 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= 27.3 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Uncompressed = **409.6 sec (≈6.83 min)**; Compressed = **27.3 sec**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 15x speedup is why **lossy compression (JPEG)** is preferred for radiology in low-bandwidth rural telemedicine, despite minor diagnostic-quality loss.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Telemedicine Types

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Real-Time | Remote Monitoring |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth Need** | Low to Medium | High | Medium (continuous) |
| **Latency Tolerance** | High (minutes OK) | Very Low (<200 ms) | Medium |
| **Data Type** | Images, reports | Audio + Video | Vital signs streams |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low | High | Medium (wearables) |
| **Specialist Availability** | Async (flexible) | Sync (must be present) | Async (alerts) |
| **Example** | Teleradiology | eSanjeevani OPD | Remote ICU |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: General Telemedicine System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Site PHC Clinic] --> B[Biomedical Sensors ECG BP SpO2 Stethoscope]
    B --> C[Data Acquisition Unit ADC]
    C --> D[Local Processing Compression Encryption]
    D --> E[Network Gateway Internet 4G 5G Satellite]
    E --> F[Secure Cloud Server EHR Storage]
    F --> G[Specialist Hospital End]
    G --> H[Clinician Review Decision]
    H --> I[Feedback Prescription]
    I --> E
    E --> J[Patient Receives Advice]
    J --> A
    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Telemedicine. List any four benefits of telemedicine.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Telemedicine is the use of information and communication technologies (ICT) by healthcare professionals to deliver healthcare services across geographic distance, for diagnosis, treatment, prevention of disease, and continuing education of healthcare providers. *(WHO 1997)*

**Four Benefits:**
1. **Geographic Accessibility:** Reaches patients in remote/rural areas without travel.
2. **Cost-Effectiveness:** Reduces patient travel costs, hospital infrastructure burden.
3. **Time Efficiency:** Faster specialist consultation, reduced waiting time.
4. **Continuity of Care:** Enables chronic disease monitoring and follow-up.
5. *(Any other valid point: improved specialist access, disaster response, etc.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Synchronous and Asynchronous telemedicine with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Synchronous (Real-Time) | Asynchronous (Store-and-Forward) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Live interaction between patient and doctor | Patient data is stored and sent to specialist for later review |
| **Time** | Real-time | Time-delayed |
| **Technology** | Video conferencing, live streaming | Email, file transfer, image upload |
| **Bandwidth** | High | Low to medium |
| **Examples** | Video consultation via eSanjeevani, Tele-ICU live monitoring | Teleradiology (X-ray sent for later review), Telepathology |
| **Specialist Availability** | Must be present live | Can review at convenience |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **block diagram of a general telemedicine system** with all major functional blocks. Describe the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of telemedicine** based on (i) timing, (ii) medical specialty, and (iii) network used. Give one example for each category. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Functional Block Description:**

1. **Patient Site / Clinical End:** The location of the patient — typically a PHC, rural clinic, or home. Houses the patient, attending nurse, and primary medical equipment.

2. **Biomedical Sensors / Data Acquisition Instruments:** Devices like ECG, pulse oximeter, digital stethoscope, glucometer, BP monitor. These sense physiological signals and produce analog electrical outputs.

3. **Data Acquisition Unit (DAQ) / ADC:** Converts the analog physiological signals into digital form using an Analog-to-Digital Converter. Sampling theorem is applied here.

4. **Local Processing Unit:** Performs **compression** (e.g., JPEG, MPEG-4), **encryption** (AES-256, TLS 1.3), and stores data temporarily before transmission.

5. **Network Gateway / Communication Link:** Routes digital data over **internet, 4G/5G, satellite (VSAT), or Wi-Fi** to the central server or directly to the specialist site.

6. **Central Server / Cloud Storage:** Stores patient data in **Electronic Health Record (EHR)** format using standards like **HL7 / DICOM**. Ensures data integrity and availability.

7. **Specialist End / Hospital Site:** Where the consulting doctor is located. The data is decrypted and presented to the clinician.

8. **Clinician Review and Decision Support:** The specialist analyzes data (with possible AI assistance) and makes a diagnostic and therapeutic decision.

9. **Feedback / Prescription Loop:** The diagnosis, prescription, and advice are transmitted back through the same network to the patient site and stored in the EHR.

---

**(b) Classification of Telemedicine (7 marks)**

**(i) Based on Timing:**
- **Store-and-Forward (Asynchronous):** Patient data is collected, stored, and forwarded to specialist later. *Example: Teleradiology — X-ray image emailed for review.*
- **Real-Time (Synchronous):** Live consultation between patient and doctor. *Example: Video consultation via eSanjeevani.*
- **Remote Monitoring:** Continuous data streaming from patient wearables. *Example: Remote ICU vital sign monitoring.*

**(ii) Based on Medical Specialty:**
- **Teleradiology** — Radiology images (X-ray, CT, MRI)
- **Telecardiology** — ECG, echocardiogram
- **Telepathology** — Histopathology slide images
- **Telepsychiatry** — Mental health video sessions
- **Tele-ICU** — Critical care at distance
- **Teledermatology** — Skin lesion images

**(iii) Based on Network Used:**
- **PSTN / ISDN** — Early low-bandwidth systems
- **Broadband Internet** — Modern high-speed applications
- **3G / 4G / 5G Mobile** — Portable, smartphone-based
- **Satellite (VSAT)** — Remote/inaccessible areas, ships, aircraft
- **Wi-Fi / LAN** — Hospital-internal telemedicine

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe the **WHO definition of telemedicine** in detail. Explain **any five components** required for the successful implementation of a telemedicine system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An ECG system samples **8 channels** at **1000 Hz** with **16-bit resolution** for a duration of **60 seconds**. Calculate: (i) Total raw data generated in MB. (ii) Size after 20:1 compression. (iii) Transmission time over a **2 Mbps** connection (post-compression). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) WHO Definition & Components (7 marks)**

**Definition:** As per the World Health Organization (1997), telemedicine is *"The delivery of healthcare services, where distance is a critical factor, by healthcare professionals using information and communication technologies (ICT) for the exchange of valid information for the diagnosis, treatment and prevention of disease and injuries, research and evaluation, and for the continuing education of healthcare providers, all in the interests of advancing the health of individuals and their communities."*

**Five Key Components:**

1. **Hardware / Biomedical Instruments:** ECG, EEG, vital sign monitors, imaging equipment at patient site, and display/review systems at specialist site.

2. **Software / Information Systems:** EHR systems, DICOM viewers, HL7 messaging platforms, encryption/decryption software.

3. **Communication Network:** Reliable, high-bandwidth, low-latency network — broadband internet, 4G/5G, satellite, or Wi-Fi.

4. **Human Resources:** Trained doctors, specialists, nurses, paramedics, biomedical engineers, and IT support staff at both ends.

5. **Protocols & Legal Framework:** Standard operating procedures, ethical guidelines, data privacy laws (HIPAA, DISHA), licensing agreements, and reimbursement policies.

6. *(Bonus point: Patient-side awareness and digital literacy.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Number of channels: $N = 8$
- Sampling rate: $f_s = 1000$ Hz
- Bits per sample: $n = 16$ bits
- Duration: $T = 60$ seconds
- Compression ratio: $CR = 20:1$
- Bandwidth: $B = 2$ Mbps

**Step 1: Calculate Data Rate (R)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= f_s \times n \times N \\[4pt]
R &= 1000 \times 16 \times 8 \\[4pt]
R &= 128{,}000 \text{ bits/second} = 128 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Raw Data in MB (Part i)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Data} &= R \times T \\[4pt]
&= 128{,}000 \times 60 \\[4pt]
&= 7{,}680{,}000 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
\text{In Bytes} &= \frac{7{,}680{,}000}{8} = 960{,}000 \text{ Bytes} \\[4pt]
\text{In MB} &= \frac{960{,}000}{10^6} = 0.96 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Compressed Size (Part ii)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed Size} &= \frac{0.96 \text{ MB}}{20} = 0.048 \text{ MB} \; (\approx 48 \text{ KB})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Transmission Time (Part iii)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed Size in bits} &= 0.048 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 384{,}000 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
T_t &= \frac{384{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.192 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) **0.96 MB** raw data
- (ii) **0.048 MB** compressed
- (iii) **0.192 seconds** transmission time

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert bits to bytes (÷ 8) or bytes to MB (÷ 10⁶ or ÷ 1024²). Always write the unit at every step.
> 2. **Missing the WHO Year:** The telemedicine definition must reference **WHO 1997** explicitly. Quoting an older or modified version leads to a ½ mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Telemedicine with Telehealth:** Telemedicine is *clinical* only; Telehealth includes non-clinical (education, administration). Marks lost if definitions are swapped.
> 4. **Skipping Block Diagram in 7-mark Questions:** If the question asks for "block diagram of telemedicine system" and you only describe it in text, expect a **2–3 mark deduction**. Always include a labeled diagram.
> 5. **Forgetting "Real-World Example":** Every classification point must include an example (e.g., eSanjeevani for India, NASA for space). Generic answers lose impact marks.
> 6. **Compression Ratio Direction:** CR = Original / Compressed, **NOT** the reverse. Writing it wrong gives a numerically incorrect final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Read This Section 3 Times Before the Exam.**

- ✅ **WHO 1997 Definition** of Telemedicine — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Technology, People, Process.
- ✅ **Three Timing Types:** Store-and-Forward (Async), Real-Time (Sync), Remote Monitoring.
- ✅ **Key Specialty Branches:** Teleradiology, Telecardiology, Telepathology, Telepsychiatry, Tele-ICU.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** **HL7** (textual data), **DICOM** (medical images).
- ✅ **Key Indian Platform:** **eSanjeevani** — Government of India's national telemedicine service.
- ✅ **Key Numerical Formulas:**
  - $R = f_s \times n \times N$
  - $f_{s,\min} = 2 \times f_{max}$ (Nyquist)
  - $T_t = \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$
- ✅ **Typical Values:** ECG sampling 250–500 Hz, Video bandwidth 384 kbps+, Latency < 200 ms.
- ✅ **Major Differences:** Telemedicine (clinical) vs. Telehealth (broader); Synchronous (live) vs. Asynchronous (delayed).
- ✅ **Real-World Examples:** eSanjeevani, NASA space medicine, tele-ECG in Kerala PHCs, 2018 Kerala floods telemedicine deployment.
- ✅ **Security Standards:** Encryption (AES-256), TLS protocols, HIPAA (US), DISHA (India).
- ✅ **Compression Use Case:** Lossy (JPEG) for images — saves bandwidth; Lossless preferred for ECG/EEG (clinical accuracy).
- ✅ **Block Diagram Order:** Patient → Sensors → ADC → Processing → Network → Server → Specialist → Feedback.
